Fortunately,  however,  Professor  Schachterle  was  there  and 
volunteered  his  service  to  help  me  through  the  evening.  He 
ated,  more  coherently,  what  I  had  said  and  asked  if  there 

any  questions.  He  tried  to  make  Larry  read  his  short 
'y,  to  start  a  discussion  about  creative  writing.  Larry 
ised,  but  instead  volunteered  to  tell  what  a  short  story 
nt   to    him.    He   spoke    negatively    about   William    Carlos 


Williams's  "The  Use  of  Force,"  the  only  story  in  the  text  he 
had  read.  Since  the  story  was  only  four  pages  long,  1  asked 
Larry  to  read  it  aloud.  He  did,  and  a  good  discussion  ensued. 

After  this  discussion,  Larry  asked  if  Professor  Schachterle 
would  continue  with  the  class  and  if  I  would  help  him  end  his 
short  story.  His  short  story  is  by  no  means  a  masterpiece,  but 
is  still  quite  good  in  that  it  evokes  the  reader's  sympathy  for 
the  protagonist. 

On  my  return  after  break,  the  students  were  writing  about 
what  they  wanted  from  the  course.  I  collected  these  writing 
samples  at  the  end  of  class.  Meeting  eleven  students  was  a 
frightening  experience,  but  a  good  lesson:  I  was  not  prepared 
for  the  unexpected  although  I  should  have  learned  by  now  to 
be  prepared.  Worse  than  that,  I  panicked  and  gave  up.  A 
teacher  should  always  have  on  reserve  a  few  relevant  topics  for 
discussion  to  utilize  during  lulls  in  classroom  conversation. 
Quite  often  these  ideas  and  discussions  can  be  very  informa- 
tive. Professor  Schachterle  advised  me  to  make  a  list  of  these 
things  and  incorporate  them  in  my  journal.  Those  he  sug- 
gested, along  with  others  I  have  thought  of,  are  as  follows: 

1.  What  is  Literature? 

2.  Why  do  writers  write? 

3.  For  whom  do  writers  write? 

4.  Is  what  they  write  the  truth? 

5.  Is  it  open  to  criticism? 

6.  What  do  writers  seem  to  write  about  most?  Why? 

7.  What   is  the  difference  between  a  short  story  writer 
and  an  orator;  a  clergyman;  a  poet? 

8.  What   are   the   different   ways   of  telling   a   story  — 
(points  of  view)?  Why  choose  one  over  the  other? 

9.  What  is  the  importance  of  writing? 

10.  What  are  some  of  the  problems  of  writing? 

11.  Name  a  famous  (favorite)  writer  —  What  about  him; 
his  style?  the  period? 

12.  Ask  students  to  write  on  a  topic  of  their  choice. 

13.  Have  a  good,  brief,  short  story  or  poem  handy  to  read 
aloud  and  discuss. 

Many  of  these  topics  appear  to  have  obvious  answers  but 
many  times  the  course  of  the  discussion  of  one  topic  will  lead 
to  another  topic.  I  must  reiterate  that  this  manner  of 
discussion  should  only  be  used  as  a  last  resort.  By  no  means 
should  the  teacher  rely  on  the  students  to  carry  on  the 
instruction:  students  are  more  at  ease  if  they  are  told  facts, 
and  will  at  times  be  unreceptive  to  venturing  their  own 
thoughts. 


There  came  a  point  while  I  was  reading 
a  poem  aloud,  where  one  student  was 
trying  to  burn  another's  clothes  with  a 
cigarette.  Everyone  was  laughing. 


Tuesday,  October  3 

A  few  uninterested  students  did  not  return  tonight.  Professor 
Schachterle  and  I  were  able  to  secure  five  copies  of  the  texts 
from  other  WPI  professors  and  friends. 

Those  students  who  had  books  were  prepared  for  the 
discussion  of  "Impulse."  A  black  student  named  Eddie  wanted 
to  read  to  the  class  some  poetry  he  had  written  that  day.  He 
did:  the  poetry  was  controversial.  One  poem  related  to  bad 
treatment  of  the  blacks,  another  to  his  escape  from  the 
problems  of  being  black  through  drugs,  and  the  third  of  how 
"laws  were  written  by  whites -for  whites."  The  poetry  was 
good,  and  very  moving.  There  was  no  student  response, 
however,  for  fear  of  being  judged  improperly  by  other 
inmates.  A  person  disagreeing  that  blacks  are  maltreated,  for 
example,  feared  the  possible  response  from  the  black  popu- 
lation in  jail  if  his  position  were  made  known. 

Eddie's  design  was  to  see  how  much  sympathy  he  had 
among  his  classmates.  When  he  found  he  had  none,  he  felt 
uneasy  and  decided  he  could  not  get  what  he  wanted  out  of 
the  course.  He  left. 

I  now  have  eight  students  and  enough  books  for  all  of 
them.  I  handed  out  the  books  and  assigned  two  stories  to  read 
for  Monday.  1  also  mentioned  that  I  had  read  their  writing 
samples  and  that  I  would  assign  a  writing  practice  so  that  we 
could  overcome  their  elementary  weaknesses. 


Thursday,  October  5 

Discussion  went  well  tonight.  Students  reacted  enthusiastically 
to  "Torch  Song"  and  "Virga  Vay  and  Allan  Cedar"  —  more  to 
the  first  than  the  second.  One  student,  Rich,  shows  consider- 
able intelligence  —  he  has  completed  l'/i  semesters  of  college 
and  reads  various  college  texts  while  he  is  in  prison.  He  began  a 
discussion  of  the  psychology  of  Cheever's  characters  and  asked 
if  Joan  in  "Torch  Song"  were  actually  a  psychic  character,  or 
if  her  existence  were  merely  symbolic. 

After  break,  we  talked  about  prison  life.  It  seems  as  if  only 
the  strong  survive  here.  1  assigned  three  stories  for  the 
weekend,  and  asked  the  students  to  choose  one  story  out  of  all 
those  we  had  read,  and  to  write  about  what  they  thought  the 
author's  point  was.  1  specified  that  they  should  not  rehash  the 
story  but  examine  coherently  the  author's  idea. 


Monday,  October  9 

No  school  —  Columbus  Day  holiday 


Tuesday,  October  10 

Doctor  Charles  Heventhal  came  with  me  tonight  to  obsei

Thursday,  October  12

The  poetry  was  a  bit  more  complex  for  the  students  than  I

l  ^pendently  in  the  woods  as  opposed  to  living  in  the  city. 

\mday,  October  16 
light,  I  read  aloud  to  the  class  "The  Secret  Life  of  Walter

For  the  second  hour,  I  asked  the  students  to  write  an

For  Tuesday,  I  assigned  two  more  stories. 

esday,  October  1 7 

lust  admit  that  I  am  saddened  by  the  fact  that  this  is  my 
ct-to-last  meeting  with  the  students.  The  term  has  certainly 
:n  a  valuable  experience.  Professor  Schachterle  and  two

Hemingway  and  "You  Could  Look  it  Up"  by  Thurber.  The 
discussion  was,  as  usual,  good. 

For  the  second  hour,  although  I  had  planned  to  read  the 
class  an  Ambrose  Bierce  story,  "An  Occurrence  at  Owl  Creek 
Bridge,"  we  discussed  various  life  styles  under  the  pressures  of 
society.  It  was  altogether  informative  and  worthwhile,  and  fit 
in  well  with  the  themes  of  many  of  the  stories  we  have  been 
reading. 

For  Thursday,  I  assigned  two  more  stories. 


Thursday,  October  19 

I  arrived  at  the  jail  and  was  informed  that  school  had  been 
canceled  because  there  was  no  heat  in  the  jail.  I  volunteered  to 
come  back  Monday  to  collect  books  and  pick  up  on  loose 
ends. 

I  received  a  note  Friday  stating  that  Monday's  classes  were 
canceled  due  to  the  Veterans'  Day  holiday. 

When  I  finally  was  able  to  meet  my  class  again  on  Tuesday, 
there  was  a  film  scheduled  for  the  second  hour  of  class.  We 
just  talked  for  the  first  hour,  and  enjoyed  the  film  after  the 
break.  I  was  certain  to  say  good-by  to  each  student  in- 
dividually and  wish  him  my  best. 


Conclusion 

The  teaching  program  at  Worcester  County  Jail  was  one  of  the 
most  informative  educational  experiences  that  I  have  ever  had. 
My  seventeen  meetings  provided  me  with  a  dual  insight.  First, 
I  was  able  to  learn  more  about  living  conditions  in  a  prison, 
and  of  the  dire  need  for  prison  reforms.  The  physical  structure 
of  the  Worcester  County  Jail  is  atrocious;  the  walls  lack  paint, 
the  windows,  glass.  The  fact  that  my  last  scheduled  class  was 
canceled  due  to  lack  of  heat  in  the  jail  is  typical  of  the 
degenerated  environment  imposed  on  inmates.  I  question  how 
a  man  is  supposed  to  be  "rehabilitated"  in  such  a  forboding 
environment. 

Far  worse  than  these  environmental  discomforts,  however, 
is  the  physical  and  mental  stress  on  the  inmates.  Many  of  the 
more  aggressive  prisoners  constantly  test  their  counterparts, 
single  out  those  who  offer  only  an  emotional  response,  and 
continually  ridicule  and  torment  them.  This  atmosphere  of 
hatred  and  violence  is  so  intense  that  one  of  my  students,  a 
relatively  fearless  man  who  is  able  to  work  daily  outside  the 
jail  on  a  farm,  still  suffers  from  nervous  anxiety  and  fits  of 
depression.  The  Worcester  County  Jail  houses  inmates  serving 
up  to  two  year  sentences,  and  prisoners  transferred  in  from 


I  encountered  over  and  over  the  fact 
that  I  could  not  rely  on  long-range  plans. 
But  in  the  long  run  I  found  what  was 
accomplished  equaled  or  surpassed 
what  I  had  planned  to  accomplish. 


other  prisons  for  the  final  few  months  of  a  longer  term.  I  have 
been  told  repeatedly  that  conditions  at  this  jail  are  excellent 
compared  with  the  larger  institutions  such  as  Bridgewater  and 
Walpole.  I  could  not  imagine  what  would  have  happened  to  a 
person  such  as  Dale  had  he  been  incarcerated  with  criminal 
deviants  serving  life  terms. 

Secondly,  I  learned  quite  a  bit  about  the  process  of 
teaching.  I  was  happy  to  be  able  to  work  for  part  of  the  time 
in  a  tutorial  situation  as  well  as  part  of  the  time  with  a  larger 
class.  I  found  that  a  relaxed  atmosphere,  in  which  the  teacher 
sits  within  a  circle  of  students,  thus  becoming  part  of  the  class, 
is  an  effective  means  of  stimulating  conversation.  The  teacher 
in  this  situation  is  not  alienated  from  the  student;  the  student 
feels  on  a  more  equal  basis  with  the  teacher,  and  is 
consequently  more  apt  to  ask  questions.  The  teacher  must  not, 
however,  try  to  evoke  discussion  to  the  degree  that  the 
1Q  discussion  becomes  a  game  of  giving  correct  answers  to  the 

teacher's  initial  questions.  This  leads  to  boredom  and  disin- 
terest in  the  continuance  of  a  discussion. 

I  encountered  over  and  over  the  fact  that  I  could  not  rely 
on  long-range  plans.  The  course  of  events  often  disputed  my 
expectations,  but  in  the  long  run  I  found  what  was  accom- 
plished equaled  or  surpassed  the  learning  experience  I  had 
planned  to  accomplish.  I  had  planned,  for  example,  a  relatively 
rigid  course  of  instruction  —  giving  examinations  and  adhering 
to  a  prescribed  syllabus.  This  format  was  suggested  to  me  on 
the  basis  that  inmates  who  wanted  to  reform  themselves  would 
logically  seek  out  some  sort  of  order  to  bring  to  a  like  that 
lacked  order.  My  real  wish,  on  the  contrary,  to  conduct  a  freer 
class  in  which  the  student,  through  reading  and  questioning, 
prescribes  what  he  wants  to  learn.  I  was  quite  relieved,  then, 
when  I  saw  that  the  ordered  curriculum  would  have  been 
futile.  I  was  able  to  conduct  the  course  at  my  pleasure,  and 
judging  from  both  oral  and  written  response  the  students 
received  something  from  the  course.  The  net  result  of  my 
involvement  in  the  course  was  valuable  experience  in  teaching, 
preparation,  discipline,  and  understanding  people.  I  was  also 
able  to  read  many  interesting  short  stories;  the  discussions  of 
the  stories  provided  many  insights  I  might  have  never  received 
by  myself. 

Many  inmates  at  the  Worcester  County  Jail  are  interested  in 
continuing  their  education,  if  for  no  other  reason  than  to 
break  up  the  monotonous  routine  of  prison  life.  Captain  Bird 
and  the  inmates  are  consequently  eager  to  continue  programs 
such  as  this,  not  only  in  English  Literature,  but  in  foreign 
languages,  Mathematics,  Science,  History,  Philosophy,  etc.  The 
opportunity  can  only  be  beneficial  to  all  involved.  The  crucial 
requisite  is  interested,  competent  student  volunteers.  I — I 


lot  rain  or  snow  or  hail  or  sleet  or 
<ark  of  night  .  .  .

11 


Bill  and 

the  Brother  Machine 


by  Russell  Kaj 


X  t  all  started  around  the  end  of  my  first  year.  I  was  getting 
pretty  bored  with  just  classes  —  so  I  finally  decided  to  do 
something.  I  went  down  to  the  Worcester  Volunteer  Action 
Center  and  1  discovered  that  there  are  some  3,500  fatherless 
children  in  Worcester  County,  and  that  got  to  me." 

William  Delphos,  74,  the  21 -year-old  director  of  Student 
Programs  for  Urban  Development,  Inc.  (SPUD),  heads  up  a 
cadre  of  over  300  student  volunteers  working  in  several  major 
programs  in  the  Worcester  community.  The  project  closest  to 
Bill's  heart,  though,  is  the  Big  Brother  program. 

From  the  Volunteer  Action  Center,  Bill  found  his  way  to 
the  Worcester  branch  of  Big  Brothers  of  America.  This 
organization  was  formed  to  provide,  on  a  one-to-one  basis, 
male  companionship  —  a  father  figure  —  to  fatherless  boys. 

But  then  things  ground  to  a  halt  for  Bill.  The  Big  Brother 
organization  was  against  the  idea  of  using  college  students  as 
big  brothers  because  the  students  were  only  around  for  nine 
months  a  year  and  would  normally  be  involved  in  the  program 
for  no  more  than  two  to  four  years. 

Persistence  paid  off,  though,  and  this  was  an  idea  whose 
time  had  come.  Delphos  won  a  trial  of  what  was  called  the 
"Associate"  Big  Brother  program,  and  then  started  advertising 
at  WPI  for  volunteers.  Within  a  short  time  he  had  75 
applicants. 

The  method  used  to  pair  big  brothers  and  little  brothers  is 
a  careful  and  sometimes  slow  process  of  matching  interests 
and,  hopefully,  personalities.  First  of  all,  contact  is  made  with 
the  child's  mother,  to  ensure  that  nothing  is  against  her  wishes 
—  that  would  only  aggravate  an  already  complicated  situation, 
to  the  child's  detriment.  With  the  mother's  approval,  the  child 
is  tentatively  matched  with  a  volunteer. 

By  serving  as  a  sort  of  secretary  in  the  Big  Brother 
organization,  Delphos  was  introduced  to  the  matching  process, 
and  to  the  need  for  care  right  at  the  beginning.  Within  four 
weeks  or  so,  they  had  matched  some  forty  students  with  little 
brothers. 

Actually,  according  to  Bill,  there  is  a  unique  advantage  to 
using  college  students  as  big  brothers.  Because  of  his  age,  the 
student  is  more  like  a  real  big  brother  and  can  relate  to  his 
little  brother  in  a  way  that  is  probably  denied  to  someone 
older.  He  can  be  a  friend,  pretty  much  on  the  little  brother's 
own  level. 

Bill  worked  on  the  program  for  nearly  a  year,  matching  up 
big  and  little  brothers,  "policing"  the  volunteers  to  make  sure 
that  they  were  spending  enough  time  with  their  charges  (the 
usual  expectation  is  a  couple  of  hours,  one  visit  a  week). 


Last  May,  while  organizing  a  SPUD  banquet,  Bill  was: 
the  office  of  a  local  businessman.  Talking  with  his  secretary 
Bill  discovered  that  she  was  separated  and  had  a  twelve-yea  j 
old  son.  "Up  to  that  time,  1  had  felt  that  I  had  too  much  I 
do,  organizing  the  program,  to  take  on  a  little  brother  myselj 
But  at  this  point  I  thought  that  maybe  I  could  handle  it, ; 
least  on  a  limited  basis."  He  got  approval  from  the  Big  Brothi 
office  and  that  weekend  took  his  little  brother  to  the  WPI  gyi 
to  play  basketball,  wrestle,  and  in  general  just  enjoy  some  tim 
together. 

"You  know,  you  can  do  some  pretty  screwy  things  an 
have  fun.  When  we  were  in  the  showers,  we  started  playin 
hockey  with  a  bar  of  soap  and  our  bare  feet  —  and  we  kept 
up  for  45  minutes!"  Bill  goes  on: 

"He  talked  sports  with  me  for  a  long  time  —  talking  aboi 
the  teams,  and  big  names,  and  scores  and  things.  It  reminde 
me  of  the  way  I  used  to  talk  with  my  older  brother.  There 
just  about  the  same  age  difference." 

Athletics  and  sports  play  a  big  part  in  the  program 
because  most  boys  are  interested  in  them  and  they  form  . 
readily  accessible  common  ground.  One  of  the  first  thing 
Delphos  did  after  organizing  the  program  was  to  see  Professo 
Robert  Pritchard,  head  of  the  WPI  department  of  physic.! 
education  and  athletics,  to  arrange  for  free  passes  for  the  littli 
brothers  in  the  program.  This  enables  the  student  big  brother 
to  offer  more  to  their  little  brothers  with  no  hardship  tt 
themselves.  Free  admission  to  movies  sponsored  by  Lens  as 
Lights  Club  was  also  arranged,  along  with  meals  in  thi 
cafeteria.

'You  know,"  says  Bill,  "1  see  a  lot  of  myself  in  this 
n;ram.  If  you  hit  it  off  at  all,  then  after  a  while  your  little 
iti  hers  starts  to  reflect  you  a  little  bit  —  starts  to  show  your 
pence.  At  one  point,  I  told  my  little  brother  I  thought  I 
al  changed  a  lot  during  the  eight  months  we  had  been 
I  ther.  I  was  more  interested  in  people  and  had  been  giving 
i  aght  to  my  career  plans.  He  said  that  he  had  changed  a  lot, 
y  that  at  the  beginning  he  had  not  liked  people  very  much 
n  had  few  friends  at  school  —  but  that  just  the  previous

Tortant  since  he  talked  so  openly  about  himself.  This  was 
h  first  time  he  had  done  this. 

'We  share  a  lot  of  good  times,  my  little  brother  and  I.  And 
wstimes  I  can  do  something  for  him  that's  really  nice.  For 
ttfc.nce,  his  birthday  is  four  days  after  Christmas,  and  he  just 
ued  thirteen.  This  was  kind  of  a  not-so-pleasant  birthday 
Miim  this  year  —  no  one  seemed  to  know  what  to  get  him, 
ro  I  was  away.  Well,  he's  a  real  nut  on  basketball,  so  I  got 
it  a  ticket  to  the  Celtics  game  and  mailed  it  from  Chicago.  I 
g  I  could  have  been  there  to  see  him  open  it;  his  mother 
:t  me  he  really  lit  up.  Now  he  can't  wait  until  next 
/«nesday,  when  we're  going." 


A  short  while  after  Bill  started  the  Associate  Big  Brother 
program,  he  was  approached  by  a  student  from  Holy  Cross,  a 
leader  in  a  group  called  Student  Programs  for  Urban  Develop- 
ment, Inc.  —  SPUD  —  who  volunteered  the  backing  and 
resources  of  SPUD  in  exchange  for  Bill's  becoming  the  WPI 
representative  of  SPUD,  publicizing  its  activities  on  campus 
and  helping  recruit  new  members. 

"I  was  kind  of  leery  at  first.  .  .  .  you  get  awfully  conscious 
of  the  possibility  of  being  exploited.  .  .  .  but  it  seemed  as  if  it 
would  work  out  well  for  both  our  groups,"  remembers  Bill. 

SPUD  actually  began  at  the  College  of  the  Holy  Cross  in 
1965,  a  period  when  student  social  involvement  in  community 
affairs  was  at  a  peak,  when  if  you  were  a  student  it  was  the 
"in"  thing  to  do  —  a  successor  to  the  student-oriented  civil 
rights  movement  in  the  early  sixties,  a  predecessor  to  the 
political  activism  that  was  to  shake  the  nation  in  the  late 
sixties  because  of  the  war  in  Viet  Nam.  SPUD  started  off 
oriented  toward  urban  problems.  It  worked  with  tenants 
associations  concerned  with  public  housing,  with  tutoring 
projects,  with  a  number  of  "street"  problems. 

Gradually,  SPUD's  attention  began  focusing  on  work  with 
children  —  and  usually  children  from  the  same  urban  areas 
SPUD  had  previously  worked  in:  the  Model  Cities  area,  and 
the  Great  Brook  Valley  and  Plumley  Village  subsidized- 
housing  projects.  And  with  all  of  this,  it  outgrew  Holy  Cross 
and  brought  in  significant  participation  from  students  at  WPI, 
Clark  University,  and  other  schools  in  the  Worcester  Con- 
sortium. Naturally,  when  they  heard  about  the  growing 
Associate  Big  Brother  program,  they  were  interested  in 
affiliating  the  two. 

SPUD  (which  is  entirely  student-run)  has  some  funding  of 
its  own  -  a  small  part,  in  fact,  from  the  WPI  Student  Affairs 
budget  —  and  it  has  used  this  in  several  ways  to  benefit  the  Big 
Brother  program.  Last  year,  the  Big  Brother  association  held  a 
Christmas  party  for  all  the  participants;  SPUD  dipped  into  its 
pocket  and  furnished  Bozo  the  clown  and  a  magician  for 
entertainment.  Other  resources  are  SPUD's  two  vehicles,  which 
are  available  to  big  brothers  to  take  their  little  brothers  on 
excursions. 

When  Delphos  was  asked  how  many  students  are  involved 
in  SPUD,  he  reluctantly  said  "around  300."  He  was  quick  to 
qualify  that:  "It's  really  hard  to  say.  The  program  is  kind  of 
self-expanding.  Just  last  week  I  matched  up  a  seventh-grade 
girl  with  a  student  math  tutor.  Now  the  girl  has  a  friend  who 
would    also    like    to    be   tutored,   and   the  student   tutor  has 


arranged  for  one  of  her  friends  to  do  that.  So  that  they're  both 
really  a  part  of  SPUD,  but  I  almost  didn't  know  about  the 
second  one." 

"A  lot  of  our  volunteers  are  WPI  students;  a  lot  are  from 
Holy  Cross.  Clark  has  many  of  its  own  programs,  which  are 
organized  differently  from  SPUD's.  The  Big  Brother  program 
alone  has,  1  would  guess,  some  sixty  to  seventy  students 
involved  now.  I  think  this  will  probably  continue  to  grow  for 
at  least  another  couple  of  years  before  any  leveling-off  takes 
place." 

Big  Brothers  is  but  one  of  several  programs  included  in 
SPUD,  which  also  acts  as  a  kind  of  clearing-house  for  other 
volunteer  action  groups,  including  the  Worcester  Tenants 
Association  and  The  Great  Brook  Valley  Child  Care  Compre- 
hensive Service. 

The  other  main  project  centers  are  located  at: 

1.  Worcester  Detention  Center 

Here,  ten  volunteers  each  spend  one  night  a  week  providing 
supervision  for  swimming,  bowling,  ice-skating,  movies,  and 
other  kinds  of  recreation.  French  and  Spanish-speaking  volun- 
teers are  available  for  tutoring.  "It's  kind  of  hard  to  say  how 
many  people  SPUD  reaches,"  says  Bill.  "At  the  Detention 
Center,  no  one  is  kept  there  longer  than  fourteen  days.  .  .  the 
turnover  is  terrific.  Our  volunteers  keep  at  it,  and  they  come  in 
contact  with  hundreds  of  new  people  every  month.  Who  can 
say  how  many  in  the  several  years  this  program  has  been  in 
operation?" 

2.  Woodland  Community  School 

Volunteers  are  presently  working  in  such  areas  as  tutoring  in 
math,  reading,  and  English  as  a  second  language.  The  after- 
school  program  encompasses  such  interests  as  dramatics, 
meteorology,  cooking,  ceramics,  typing,  music,  athletics,  arts 
and  crafts,  creative  play  activities,  or  any  other  thing  which 
the  children  and  volunteers  are  interested  in. 

3.  Green  Island 

A  tutoring  program  here  involves  remedial  work  and  behavior 
modification  in  conjunction  with  the  Worcester  public  school 
system.  Also  included  are  a  drop-in  center  and  an  arts  and 
crafts  program.  A  free  breakfast  program  runs  in  conjunction 
with  this  project  in  the  Kelly  Square  area. 

4.  Worcester  Rehabilitation  Center 

For  this  program,  volunteers  are  trained  to  work  with  visually 
and  physically  handicapped  children  up  to  twelve  years  of  age. 
Working  on  a  one-to-one  basis,  the  volunteers  pick  up  in  the 
evening,  two  nights  a  week,  the  work  begun  during  the  day, 
carrying  it  along  in  the  child's  home. 


Bill  Delphos 

5.  South  Worcester 

Volunteers  provide  a  number  of  different  services  he 
tutoring,  recreational  activities,  and  special  interest  grou 
such  as  a  science  club  and  stamp  collecting. 

"The  South  Worcester  program  is  really  phenomena 
according  to  Delphos.  "A  year  ago  it  started  with  may 
twenty  kids  from  the  community.  Now  there  are  over  r 
hundred,  and  they've  had  to  go  to  split  sessions!  Bernie  Con 
(Holy  Cross,  '74),  who  is  co-director  of  SPUD,  has  don< 
really  fantastic  job  in  South  Worcester.  One  thing  that  I 
really  worked  out  well  is  to  insist  that  the  parents  of  the  k 
spend  some  time  on  a  regular  basis  with  the  program.  Some 
them  come  kind  of  grudgingly,  but  they  usually  get  turned 
by  something  and  add  considerably  to  the  value  of  I 
project." 


"You  have  to  be  consistent.  If  there's 
anything  phony,  those  kids  will  spot 
it  right  away."

E  DINNER 

i  of  the  standout  events  in  SPUD's  seven-year  history  was  a 

Rner  held  last  May.  It  served  to  announce  SPUD's  presence 
the  Worcester  community,  and  it  raised  over  $3,000  for  the 
anization.  It  was  in  large  measure  the  work  of  Bill  Delphos. 

When  the  concept  was  formulated,  Bill  had  some  definite 
as  of  how  the  dinner  should  be  handled.  "We  didn't  need 

money,  really;  we  just  wanted  to  let  people  know  about 
But  I  am  firmly  convinced  that  if  you  can  get  someone  to 

just  come  but  to  also  invest  ten  or  twenty  dollars  of  his 
n  money,  then  he  will  remember  you  a  lot  longer." 

So  the  dinner  began.  First  of  all,  Bill  got  a  hall  in  the  new 
rcester  Center  donated.  Then  he  went  to  grocery  distribu- 
:1s,  meat  packers,  wine  wholesalers,  asking  for  donations. 
')  tried  getting  all  of  them  into  the  act  by  saying  'well 
;cind-so  gave  us  three  cases  of  canned  peas,  and  we're  going 
:cbe  thanking  them  in  the  program  for  it.  Would  you  like  to 
}«  there  too?'  "  And  the  snowball  kept  rolling.  "A  rental 
ctnpany  lent  us,  free,  650  place  settings,  plus  ash  trays, 
i€ving  pieces,  the  works.  The  guy  said:  'Look,  I'm  letting  you 
*e  over  $1,800  worth  of  rental  equipment  here.  Could  you 
K  rinse  it  off  a  little  afterwards  so  it's  not  all  caked  on?' 
ml,  we  took  everything  back  to  my  fraternity  house 
ifcrwards,  and  everyone  chipped  in  and  we  washed  every- 
:lng.  We  had  racks  of  dishes  everywhere,  drying,  until  we 
:|ild  take  them  all  back!" 

j  With  all  of  this,  there  still  remained  the  task  of  getting 
pople  to  come.  Here,  Bill  used  his  tried  and  proven  tactics. 
Id  go  to  see  one  company  in  town,  and  they'd  maybe  agree 
ti  buy  a  few  seats.  And  I'd  say,  'Gee,  the  XYZ  company 
bught  a  whole  table.  You  know,  these  are  recognition  tables, 
ad  the  company's  name  will  be  put  on  it,  and  everyone  there 
(111  know  about  their  commitment  to  the  goals  of  SPUD.'  "  It 
wrked  just  fine  .  .  .  and  over  two  hundred  tickets  were  sold. 

I  After  plans  were  well  under  way,  the  SPUD  dinner  also 
trned  into  WPI's  first  interactive  project.  He  was  discussing  it 
vth  John  van  Alstyne,  dean  of  academic  advising,  one  day, 
«ien  Dean  van  Alstyne  asked:  "You're  studying  on  the  WPI 
tin.  aren't  vou?  This  is  a  perfect  example  of  an  interactive 
poject  [a  project  outside  the  student's  major  field,  which 
rates  his  studies  to  social  needs]."  And  so  it  was  that  the 
cmer,  which  Bill  was  committed  to  anyway,  turned  out  to 
flfill  one  of  his  degree  requirements.  In  order  to  meet  project 
iquirements,  Bill  had  to  devise  a  title  for  the  dinner  in  25 
liters  or  less.  The  result:  Selling  a  Share  in  Youth. 


Bill  Delphos  has  obviously  already  made  a  mark  at  WPI. 
Majoring  in  business,  his  primary  interest  now  is  in  organiza- 
tional structure,  and  he  intends  for  his  qualifying  project  in  his 
major  field  to  do  a  study  of  institutional  effectiveness  among 
small  New  England  colleges.  Later  on,  he  would  like  to  look  at 
the  effects  of  Russian  technology  on  the  fishing  community  of 
Gloucester,  Massachusetts.  He  has  been  thinking  of  going  to 
England  next  year  to  study.  .  .  and  maybe  even  to  Russia  (he 
taught  himself  Russian  a  few  years  ago).  Bill  is  the  son  of 
Philip  R.  Delphos,  '26,  a  WPI  trustee  emeritus  who  lives  in 
Falmouth,  Massachusetts. 

"While  I'm  not  quite  sure  just  what  I  want  to  do,  I  have 
decided  that  I  am  going  to  play  the  organizational  game  for  a 
while,"  Bill  says.  Do  you  suppose  we  ought  to  warn  the 
Russians  he's  coming?  LJ

RICHARD  J.  DEARBORN!.  '03 

Richard  J.  Dearborn  died  November  20  in 
Morristown,  New  Jersey.  He  was  90. 

Mr.  Dearborn,  who  was  born  in  Man- 
chester, N.H.,  graduated  as  an  electrical 
engineer  from  WPI  and  later  received  his 
law  degree  from  Chicago  School  of  Law.  A 
retired  patent  attorney,  he  had  been  a  partner 
in  the  law  firm  of  Cooper,  Dunham,  Dear- 
born, and  Henninger. 

For  a  number  of  years  he  was  associated 
with  Texaco-lnc,  and  had  been  president 
and  director  of  many  of  the  firm's  affiliates. 

A  member  of  the  American  Bar  Associa- 
tion and  the  American  Patent  Law  Associa- 
tion, Mr.  Dearborn  was  the  author  of 
"Patents".  He  was  also  active  in  the  Alumni 
Association  and  at  one  time  served  as  vice 
president  of  the  Pittsburgh  chapter.  In  1963 
he  received  the  Robert  H.  Goddard  Award 
from  the  association  in  recognition  of  his 
professional  achievements. 

EDWIN  M.  ROBERTS,  '04 

Edwin  M.  Roberts,  92,  died  on  October  18, 
1972  in  Hyannis,  Massachusetts. 

A  native  of  Andover,  he  graduated  as  a 
mechanical  engineer  from  WPI  in  1904. 
From  1922  until  his  retirement  in  1949  he 
was  a  teacher  of  industrial  arts  in  Worcester 
high  schools.  Earlier  he  had  taught  at 
Lebanon,  N.H.;  Governor  Dummer  Acad- 
emy; Stamford,  Conn.;  and  at  Holyoke  and 
Lowell,  Mass. 

Following  World  War  I  he  joined  the  re- 
habilitation programs  at  Saranac  Lake,  N.Y., 
as  a  Veteran's  Bureau  training  officer. 

SIDNEY  W.  FARNSWORTH,  '06 

Sidney  W.  Farnsworth,  a  WPI  trustee 
emeritus,  died  in  December  in  New  Jersey. 

The  retired  chairman  of  the  board  of 
Torrington  (Conn.)  Mfg.  Co.,  he  was  born 
in  Lancaster,  Mass.  on  October  23,  1886. 
After  receiving  his  BS  in  electrical  engineer- 
ing from  WPI,  he  became  associated  with 
Underwriters  Laboratory,  Inc.,  Chicago,  and 
later  with  Westinghouse  Electric  in  Pitts- 
burgh. In  1917  he  became  an  associate 
scientific  attache  serving  the  American  Em- 
bassy in  London. 

After  holding  positions  as  chief  engineer 
for  the  U.S.  Post  Office  Department,  Wash- 
ington. D.C.,  and  vice  president  of  Case- 
Pomeroy  &  Company,  New  York  City,  he 
created  the  New  York  investment  and 
management  firm  of  S.  W.  Farnsworth,  Inc. 


He  also  became  president  of  Amos  L.  Be 
Company,  Inc.,  served  with  the  OPM 
Washington,  D.C.,  and  acted  as  a  membei 
the  Navy  Price  Adjustment  Board  in  N' 
York. 

In  1945  he  joined  the  Torrington  Compa 
as  president  and  in  1953  he  became  cha 
man  of  the  board. 

Mr.  Farnsworth,  who  was  a  member 
Alpha  Tau  Omega  and  Sigma  Xi,  complet 
the  full  four-year  course  at  WPI  at  the  a 
of  19,  the  youngest  graduate  ever  record 
at  the  college.  From  1952  to  1962  he  w 
an  active  trustee.  In  1967  he  received 
honorary  doctorate  of  engineering  degi 
from  WPI. 

PERCY  C.  SMITH,  '07 

Percy  C.  Smith  died  in  East  Orange,  Nf 
Jersey  on  September  7,  1972. 

Mr.  Smith,  who  was  born  on  Feb.  7,  18i 
at  Amherst,  Mass.,  studied  electrical  eni 
neering  at  WPI.  Following  graduation 
became  associated  with  the  Ohio  Bra 
Company,  Mansfield,  Ohio,  the  Unit 
States  Patent  Office,  and  Roberts,  Robe 
&  Cushman,  Boston. 

For  30  years  he  was  a  patent  attorney  I 
Western  Electric  Co.  and  Bell  Telepho 
Laboratories,  New  York  City.  He  retired 
1950.

can  Chamber  of  Commerce  in  Sao  Paul 
Brazil.

f  rofessional  Engineers.  He  was  a  registered 
ressional  engineer  in  the  state  of  Massa- 
ftsetts.

PAULA.  DAVIS,  '25 

Paul  A.  Davis,  69,  died  in  Red  Bank,  New 
Jersey  last  October. 

A  Worcester  native,  he  received  his  BS  in 
electrical  engineering  from  WPI  in  1925. 
He  was  associated  with  Electronics  Asso- 
ciates, Inc.,  West  Long  Branch,  N.J.  for 
many  years  and  was  directly  involved  in  the 
formation  of  the  company.  Prior  to  his  retire- 
ment in  1968  he  held  the  position  of  coor- 
dinator for  international  marketing. 

From  1940  until  1952  he  was  employed 
as  administrative  director  of  the  New  Equip- 
ment Introductory  Staff,  Office  of  the  Chief 
Signal  Officer,  U.S.  Army,  at  the  Pentagon, 
Washington,  D.C. 

Mr.  Davis  was  formerly  president  of  the 
New  Haven  Chapter  of  the  Alumni  Associ- 
ation. 

RICHARD  P.  HOULIHAN,  '26 

Richard  P.  Houlihan,  former  president  of  the 
Gavitt  Wire  and  Cable  Co.,  died  November 
11,  1972  in  East  Brookfield,  Massachusetts. 
He  was  68. 

He  was  born  in  Springfield.  In  1926  he 
graduated  from  WPI  as  an  electrical  engineer. 

In  1936  he  joined  Gavitt  Wire  and  Cable 
Co.,  where  he  was  appointed  general 
manager  in  1954,  vice  president  in  1957, 
and  president  in  1959.  He  retired  in  January 
of  1972.  Previously  he  had  worked  for 
Westinghouse  Electric,  Erie  Resistor  Corp. 
and  United  American  Bosch  Corp. 

During  World  War  II  he  was  a  Marine 
lieutenant  colonel. 

HARRY  B.  ZACKRISON,  '26 

Harry  B.  Zackrison,  68,  who  retired  two 
years  ago  as  chief  of  the  engineering 
division  for  military  construction  of  the 
Army  Corps  of  Engineers,  died  November 
16,  1972. 

Mr.  Zackrison  was  a  pioneer  in  improved 
design  of  military  construction.  In  1970  The 
American  Concrete  Institute  awarded  him 
the  Turner  Gold  Medal  in  recognition  of  his 
outstanding  contributions.  He  also  received 
the    1970    F.    Stuart    Fitzpatrick    Memorial 


Award  from  the  leading  professional  societies 
in  the  construction  industry.  It  was  the  first 
time  the  Fitzpatrick  award  was  made  to  an 
engineer  and  government  official. 

In  1969  he  was  named  by  Engineering 
News-Record  as  one  of  the  outstanding 
men  in  construction  for  his  role  in  establish- 
ing the  Corps  Construction  Engineering  Re- 
search Laboratory.  His  achievements  have 
also  been  cited  by  Lt.  Gen.  F.  J.  Clarke, 
chief  of  engineers. 

He  was  born  in  Bridgeport,  Conn.,  and 
graduated  from  WPI  as  a  civil  engineer.  He 
was  a  member  of  Tau  Beta  Pi  and  Sigma  Xi. 


EVERETT  D.  GIBBS,  '28 

Everett  D.  Gibbs,  vice  president  of  Com- 
munication Associates,  Inc.,  Huntington 
Station,  New  York,  passed  away  January 
13,1972. 

He  was  born  in  Clinton,  Mass.,  on  May 
14,  1907.  In  1928  he  received  his  BS  in 
electrical  engineering  from  WPI  where  he 
was  a  member  of  Alpha  Tau  Omega  Fra- 
ternity. 

Prior  to  joining  the  Associates  in  1  959, 
he  was  affiliated  with  Erco  Radio  Labs, 
Garden  City,  N.Y.;  Radio  Receptor,  Inc., 
New  York  City;  and  Bendix  Radio  Corp. 

Mr.  Gibbs  was  a  member  of  IRE  and  the 
U.S.  Power  Squadron. 

CHARLES  L.  LAVEZZOLI,  '38 

Charles  L.  Lavezzoli  passed  away  in  Connect- 
icut on  June  20,  1972,  at  the  age  of  56. 

Born  in  Chester,  Conn.,  he  later  graduated 
from  WPI  with  a  degree  in  civil  engineering. 
He  served  as  a  bridge  engineer  with  the 
Connecticut  State  Highway  Department 
and  had  been  with  the  department  since 
1938. 

He  was  a  member  of  Phi  Kappa  Theta 
and  Sigma  Xi. 

EDWARD  J.  BOROWIEC,  '70 

Navy  Ensign  Edward  J.  Borowiec,  23,  was 
killed  in  a  bus-car  accident  in  the  Philippine 
Islands  on  October  19,  1972. 

He  was  born  in  Chicopee,  Mass.,  and 
later  received  his  bachelor  of  science  degree 
in  civil  engineering  from  WPI.  He  enlisted 
in  the  Navy  in  1970  and  received  his  com- 
mission at  Newport,  R.I.,  in  July  of  1971. 

Ensign  Borowiec,  who  was  only  recently 
assigned  to  the  Philippine  Islands,  was  a 
member  of  Phi  Kappa  Theta  Fraternity.

replace  Poseidon  eventually."

SKYLAB,  Space, 

and 

Harrison  Brown 


HARRISON  K.  BROWN,  '39,  was  not  an  adviser  for  the  elegant 
Hollywood  space  epic  "2001",  but  he  might  well  have  been.  For 
more  than  a  decade  his  life  has  been  dedicated  to  the  U.S.  space 
program.  His  accomplishments  with  the  National  Aeronautics  Space 
Administration,  culminating  in  the  successful  flight  of  Apollo  11, 
were  noted  on  the  floor  of  Congress  and  written  into  the  Congres- 
sional Record  (July  17,  1969). 

From  1961  until  his  retirement  in  July  of  1972,  Brown  was  with 
NASA's  Marshall  Space  Flight  Center,  Huntsville,  Ala.  At  Huntsville 
he  served  as  systems  engineering  manager — Agena  launch  vehicles; 
study  manager — advanced  space  systems;  and  systems  engineer — 
space  shuttle  propulsion  systems. 

Although  retired,  he  still  takes  a  lively  interest  in  what  is  going  on 
or  "up"  in  the  space  program.  Currently  he  is  enthusiastic  about 
Skylab,  the  manned  orbital  scientific  space  station  which  is  slated 
for  launching  in  May. 

Harrison  Brown  detailed  his  role  in  the  space  program  in  a  talk  to 
the  Tech  Old-Timers: 

"In  1961,  when  NASA  was  starting  to  orbit  unsophisticated 
satellites  and  only  thinking  about  the  moon  and  planetary  missions, 
I  joined  the  Agency  in  Huntsville  which  was  responsible  for  guidance 
of  the  Atlas/ Agena.  Our  first  mission  was  the  Ranger  which  crash 
landed  an  instrumentation  package  on  the  moon.  The  package  was 
encased  in  crushable  material  that  absorbed  the  shock  and  broke 
away  to  expose  the  instrumentation.  The  instruments  were  weighted 
to  roll  into  a  position  that  would  point  their  antennae  towards  the 
earth.  It  took  seven  failures,  but  three  successful  Ranger  missions 


provided  NASA  with  the  needed  preliminary  lunar  surface  data  fora 
manned  landing. 

"The  Atlas/Agena  also  launched  the  Mariner  I  mission,  a  Venus 
fly-by  that  came  close  enough  to  the  planet  to  prove  its  surface  was 
too  hot  for  man  (800°F)  and  its  atmosphere  so  dense  that  it  would 
crush  him  to  death  regardless  of  protective  coating.  This  mission  re- 
quired three  months  of  agony  as  the  space  craft  developed  numerous 
internal  problems  in  transit  all  of  which  by  some  miracle,  cor- 
rected themselves.  One  example  was  the  light  sensor  that  locked 
onto  the  moon  instead  of  the  earth  which  would  have  prevented  data 
transmission  from  the  planet.  However  a  micrometeoroid  collided 
with  the  space  craft,  broke  the  moon  lock,  and  after  a  search,  it 
correctly  locked  onto  the  earth.  The  worry  time  was  increased  to  six 
months  when  a  Mars/Mariner  fly-by  mission  determined  the  planet 
to  be  desolate  and  practically  devoid  of  atmosphere,  but  capable  ol 
manned  landings  similar  to  the  moon. 

"In  1 963  I  was  given  the  responsibility  to  define  a  system  to  make 
practical  use  of  the  developed  Apollo  hardware.  Since  it  was  obvious 
that  the  moon  has  no  practical  civilian  or  military  use  because  of  its 
great  distance,  we  concentrated  on  earth  orbit.  The  system  originally 
proposed  was  an  orbiting  workshop  using  the  empty  liquid  hydrogen 
tank  of  the  second  stage  of  the  small  Saturn  after  it  had  burned  itself 
into  a  270  mile  high  orbit.  The  furnishings  and  test  equipment, 
packaged  in  a  box  on  the  forward  end  of  the  vehicle,  would  be 
moved  into  the  workshop  and  set  up  by  the  three  astronauts  who 
would  come  up  on  a  second  Saturn  flight  in  an  Apollo  Command 
Module.  Later  studies  proved  it  to  be  more  practical  to  build  the 
workshop  on  the  ground  and  launch  it  with  the  large  Saturn  V  which 
is  used  for  the  Apollo  missions.  At  this  point  the  program  was  named 
'Skylab'. 

"The  Skylab  workshop  is  built  into  the  liquid  hydrogen  fuel  tank 
of  a  surplus  Saturn  SIVb  stage  from  which  all  propulsion  and  control 
systems  have  been  removed.  It  is  twenty-two  feet  in  diameter  and 
has  two  levels.  The  first  level  is  for  astronaut  living  and  contains 
kitchen,  dining  and  toilet  facilities.  The  crew  will  eat  earth-type  food 
prepared  aboard.  They  will  have  toilet  facilities  similar  to  those  on 
earth  and  have  a  shower  bath,  both  of  which  required  ingenious 
design  because  of  weightlessness.  The  men  sleep  in  a  vertical  posi-' 


H  strapped  to  a  slab-type  bed  which  is  best  in  zero  gravity.  They 
ilso  have  an  extensive  exercise  program  to  combat  the  "bed- 
n"  effect  of  long-time  weightlessness. 

he  second  level  contains  most  of  the  scientific  instrumentation 
s  the  work  area.  Power  is  provided  by  a  bank  of  solar  cells  that 
art  the  sun's  energy  into  electrical  power.  Batteries  are  installed 
Dwer  when  the  Skylab  is  in  the  earth's  shadow.  In  addition  to 
asic  workshop  there  is  an  airlock  to  permit  excursions  outside 
ehicle,  and  a  docking  adapter  to  which  the  Command  Module 
the  three  astronauts  will  dock.  Also  attached  to  the  cluster  is 
,TM  (Apollo  Telescope  Module)  which  consists  of  a  group  of 
:opes  mounted  in  a  common  barrel  that  will  be  used  for  study- 
haracteristics  of  the  sun. 

here  will  be  three  missions  on  the  Skylab  each  involving  a 
ate  crew  of  three  astronauts.  The  first  mission  will  last  28  days 
:he  other  two  56  days  with  the  Skylab  shut  down  between 
ons.  The  first  mission  and  launch  of  the  Skylab  cluster  is  scheduled 
oring  1973. 

he  first  mission  will  stress  medical  aspects  primarily  to  deter- 
the  effect  on  the  astronauts  of  that  length  of  time  in  weightless- 
The  first  crew  will  consist  of  a  medical  doctor,  an  engineer, 
in  experienced  pilot. 

he  second  mission  of  56  days  will  also  study  the  medical 
:ts  under  a  larger  time  of  weightlessness,  but  will  concentrate 
jdying  the  sun  using  the  ATM.  Its  crew  will  also  include  a  medical 
>r,  a  technical  man,  and  a  pilot. 


"Since  the  Skylab  will  fly  over  90  percent  of  the  earth's  populated 
areas,  the  third  mission  will  concentrate  on  determining  usefulness 
of  space  for  solving  earth's  problems  in  such  areas  as  agriculture, 
pollution,  environmental  control,  weather  prediction,  sea  state  warn- 
ings, fish  concentration,  navigation,  etc. 

"In  mid  1974  the  Skylab  will  be  abandoned  and  its  orbit  will 
gradually  decay  because  of  atmospheric  impact.  It  will  eventually 
reenter  and  burn  up  in  the  earth's  atmosphere." 

Brown  didn't  start  out  his  career  with  the  moon  as  his  goal.  Follow- 
ing graduation  from  WPI,  he  worked  for  Narragansett  Electric  Co., 
Providence,  R.I.,  as  a  junior  electrical  engineer.  Later  he  was  con- 
cerned with  ship  design  at  Mare  Island  Naval  Shipyard,  Vallejo, 
Calif.  From  1946  to  1957  he  was  responsible  for  the  complete  elec- 
trical system  and  equipment  on  submarines  in  the  U.S.  fleet  while 
he  was  with  the  Navy  Department,  Bureau  of  Ships,  Washington, 
D.C.  In  1 957  he  joined  the  Army  Rocket  and  Guided  Missile  Agency 
in  Huntsville,  Ala.,  where  he  was  electronics  engineer  on  the  Talos 
and  Mauler  missile  systems. 

Brown  is  the  son  of  Harrison  G.  Brown,  '12,  of  Worcester.  The 
senior  Brown  was  fourteen  years  old  when  the  Wright  brothers 
took  off  in  the  first  flimsy  but  successful  powered  airplane  at  Kitty 
Hawk,  N.C.,  back  in  1903. 

His  son,  Harrison  K.,  who  has  been  instrumental  in  getting  our 
astronauts  to  the  moon  and  back,  has  just  become  a  brand  new 
grandfather.  What  celestial  horizons  will  that  grandchild  be  able  to 
reach  in,  say,  the  year  2001?  j

we'll  be  there  in  spirit. 

The  Norton  Spirit. 


That's  the  name  we've  chosen 
forthe  championship  race  car  we'll 
be  campaigning  on  some  of  the 
world's  toughest  testing  grounds... 
a  lean,  tough  rolling  projectile 
crammed  to  the  throttle  with  850 
horses  of  double  overhead  cam 
turbo-super-charged  power! 

Sponsored  by  various  divisions 
of  Norton  Company  whose  products 
are  closely  linked  to  the  manufac- 
ture and  fabrication  of  most  auto- 
motive parts,  this  sleek  racing 


Norton  grinding  wheels  are 

depended  on  for  precision 

grinding  and  finishing  of  critical 

engine  components,  transmission 

parts,  drive  shafts,  steering  gear 

and  front  wheel  suspension  parts. 

Norton  coated  abrasive  belts, 
sheets  and  discs  play  an 
important  part  in  grinding  brake 
shoes,  crankshaft  lapping  and  in 
the  preparation  and  finishing  of 
all  types  of  metal  and  fiberglass 
automotive  panels. 

Because  they  grip  curved  and 
irregular  surfaces  tightly  and 
strip  off  quickly  and  cleanly, 
Norton  pressure  sensitive  mask- 
ing tapes  are  used  extensively 
in  the  preparation  and  final 
painting  of  automotive  bodies. 


machine  will  hit  the  U.S. A.C.  cham- 
pionship trail  from  Indianapolis  to 
Ontario,  from  the  Pocono  500  to 
the  Michigan  200. 

Our  entry  in  this  internationally 
renowned  sports  event  underscores 
the  important  role  Norton  products 
play  in  the  automotive  industry.  It 
also  reflects  the  combined  talents, 
ski  I  Is  and  competitive  spi  rit  of 
Norton's  1 8,000  employees.  It  is  this 
same  spirit  of  endeavor  and  a 
genuine  desire  to  excel  that  has 


made  Norton  the  world's  largest 
and  best  known  manufacturer  of 
abrasives  and  a  prime  supplier  of 
industrial  ceramics,  pressure- 
sensitive  tapes  and  plastics  and 
synthetics. 

Keep  your  eye  on  the  new 
Norton  Spirit...  an  exciting  symbol 
of  a  company  and  people  that  are 
on  the  move!  Norton  Company,- 
World  Headquarters,  Worcester, 
Massachusetts  0 1 606. 


Norton  industrial  ceramics, 
including  refractory  cements, 
are  widely  used  to  line  melting 
furnaces  in  which  automotive 
engine  blocks  and  other 
components  are  cast. 

Norton  abrasive  materials  are 
selected  for  their  long  life  and 
increased  productivity  on  a 
variety  of  automotive  produc- 
tion applications  including  the 
deburring  and  finishing  of  gears, 
carburetor  parts,  connecting 
rods,  ball  joints,  butterfly 
valves  and  thermostats. 

Norton  TYGON®  tubing  is 
ideal  for  low  pressure  fuel, 
vent  and  refueling  lines  where 
high  visibility  is  required.

1961 


WILLIAM  CALDER  III  has  been  appointed 
as  technical  consultant  on  the  development 
and  engineering  staff  of  the  Foxboro  Com- 
pany, Foxboro,  Mass.  In  his  new  position 
he  will  be  active  in  the  company's  focus 
on  product  safety  for  its  line  of  industrial 
process  controls.  He  joined  the  company  in 
1963  and  was  promoted  to  senior  product 
engineer  in  1969.  He  has  been  concerned 
with  electrical  codes  and  safety  on  national 
and  international  levels.  He  has  written 
papers  on  the  subject  and  is  currently  serv- 
ing as  a  U.S.  delegate  on  several  Interna- 
tional Electrotechnical  Commission  com- 
mittees relative  to  hazardous  area  operations.

1973  ENGINEERING  GRADUATES  (AE,  ME,  EE) 


Sikorsky  and  Connecticut 
...a  superb  setting  for  getting 
your  career  off  the  ground 
faster. 


The  famous  American  Shakespearean  Festival 
Theatre  in  Stratford,  Conn. 


Start  the  life  you'll  want  to  live  for  a  long  time— 
and  the  career  that  makes  it  possible— with  Sikorsky. 
In  the  picturesque  Southern  Connecticut  countryside  off 
Long  Island  Sound,  where  living  still  means  breathing 
deeply  and  being  able  to  stretch  out  to  satisfactions  that 
are  meaningful.  With  forests,  streams  and  rolling 
countryside  as  near  to  your  door  as  the  famed  Shakespeare 
Theatre  at  Stratford . . .  and  New  York  City's  attractions 
within  easy  reach. 

You  will  find  equal  stimulation  and  opportunity  in 
your  work— using  your  creative  engineering  abilities  to 
help  take  VTOL  aircraft  into  their  dynamic  future.  The 
truly  unique  capabilities  of  these  craft  are  being  increasingly 
recognized  daily. To  bring  access  where  none  existed. 
To  help  build  bridges,  dams,  oil  rigs,  transmission 
towers,  plants  and  a  host  of  other  structures,  by  hauling 
and  setting  major  components  in  place.  For  exploration, 
firefighting,  lifesaving  and  a  whole  new  range  of 
commercial  as  well  as  military  applications.  And  you  can 


look  ahead  with  confidence  to  continuing  growth  with 
Sikorsky— for  no  other  company  is  so  strongly  committed 
and  so  deeply  involved  in  the  effort  to  develop 
advanced  VTOL  technology. 

Our  ongoing  long-term  programs  offer  a  wide  range 
of  positions  for  Aeronautical,  Mechanical  and  Electrical 
Engineers.  Areas  include  DESIGN  (aircraft  structures; 
propulsion  systems;  transmissions;  rotor,  hydraulic  &  flight 
control  systems;  electrical/electronic  systems); 
TEST  and  ANALYSIS  (structural,  loads,  dynamic,  stress, 
mass  properties,  reliability/maintainability;  electrical/ 
electronic  systems;  technical  computing)  as  well  as 
MANUFACTURING  ENGINEERING  (planning,  methods, 
processes).  Salaries  are  highly  competitive,  fringe 
benefits  extremely  fine. 

For  further  information,  see  your  Placement  Director  tor 
a  copy  of  our  brochure  and  for  the  date  of  our  scheduled 
campus  interviews.  Or  write  directly  to  Mr.  Leo  J.  Shalvoy, 
Professional  Placement.

Genetic  Engineering  Revisited 

There  was  some  blank  space  in  the  October 
issue,  in  the  Feedback  section,  and  that  was 
due  to  my  procrastination  in  writing  about 
the  most  excellent  series  of  articles  in  the 
August  issue.  In  particular,  the  manner  of 
presentation  was  particularly  effective,  and 
represented  real  perspective  on  the  progress 
and  the  training  of  the  advances  now  being 
made  and  the  inevitability  of  the  change.  It 
will  challenge  all  our  thinking  to  cope  with 
this  advance  in  "revelation." 

Perhaps  you  are  not  planning  another 
version  of  this  in  another  year  or  two  (or 
whatever  rate  things  are  changing  signifi- 
cantly today  and  tomorrow),  but  it  will  be 
absolutely  a  fascinating  and  important  item 
to  help  those  of  us  following  the  progress  to 
get  such  a  comprehensive  update  in  some- 
thing like  "Genetic  Engineering  Revisited." 

George  M.  Dew/re,  '49 


How  about  "Son  of  Genetic  Engineering"? 
—Ed. 


The  New  Look 

Although  some  of  you  may  not  have  noticed  any  differ- 
ence at  all,  the  Journal's  layout  has  been  completely 
revised  with  this  issue.  Type  styles  and  sizes  are  un- 
changed, but  the  column  widths  and  margins  have  been 
increased  slightly  to  correct  some  problems  both  visual 
and  technical  that  had  appeared  in  the  old  format — 
which,  like  Topsy,  "jest  grew." 

The  new  layout  consolidates  available  white  space 
more,  providing  relief  for  the  eye,  and  helps  make  the 
placement  of  photographs  and  other  illustrations  more 
appealing. 

The  standing  departmental  headlines  have  been  rede- 
signed to  complement  each  other  better.  The  numerals  for 
classes  in  "Your  Class  and  Others"  have  been  enlarged 
and  set  in  a  different  type  style,  to  make  a  more  pleasing 
and  readable  page.  The  contents  page  has  been  exten- 
sively revamped  to  make  it  a  more  attractive  and  fitting 
introduction  to  the  magazine. 

The  Journal  logotype  has  also  been  redesigned.  Of  the 
new  nameplate  its  designer,  Lee  Rand  of  Princeton, 
Massachusetts,  says:  "What  our  eyes  expect  to  see  conflicts 
with  the  design,  creating  an  illusion  of  sorts.  The  close- 
cropped  background  tempts  us  to  try  and  'read'  the  dark 
areas  as  fetters,  but,  when  we  read  the  white  space, 
JOURNAL  appears.  Imagination  provides  the  missing  top 
and  bottom  of  each  letter.  Look  at  it  a  while  and  the  white 
letters  appear  to  float  in  front  of  the  dark  background.  The 
lettering  is  a  mix  of  capital  and  lower-case  letters  known 
as  'biform.' "

IE  STUDENT-ALUMNI   RELATIONSHIP 


WPI     Community    embraces    several    groups,    including 

nts,  faculty,  and  administration.  There  is  another,  equally 

brtant  group,  namely  alumni,  which  is  an  integral  part  of 

otal  community.  Since  most  alumni  are  not  on  campus  as 

as    the    others,    they    are    frequently    overlooked    by 

ents  and  innocently  forgotten  as  a  part  of  WPI. 

Students  usually  find  it  difficult  to  relate  to  the  fact  that 

flgday    they    are   going    to   be   alumni   of  WPI   themselves. 

Inmost  in  the  minds  of  undergraduates  are  the  thoughts  of 

Buating,  of  finding  some  form  of  placement  and  beginning 

Ir  new  endeavors,  but  few  thoughts  are  directed  towards 

iir  relationship  to  or  contact  with  WPI  once  they  graduate. 

1/  after  being   away   from   the  Hill  for  a  while  do  recent 

puates  start  to  think  of  their  roles  as  alumni.  But  this  is 

ly  too  late.  Students  should  be  aware  of  alumni,  and  the 

that  they  will  someday  join  the  Alumni  Association,  while 

'    are    still    undergraduates    on   campus.   The   attitudes   of 

y  older   alumni   towards  WPI  are  developed  during  their 

ergraduate  years.  It  is  therefore  extremely  important  that 

proper  knowledge  and  attitudes  between  the  school  and 

nni  be  inculcated  while  students  are  still  on  campus. 

In    addition   to    undergraduates   being    aware    of   alumni, 

nni  must  also  be  aware  of  students,  of  new  campus  trends, 

|  of  current  college  life.  Students  and  alumni  should  have  a 

jd  mutual  understanding,  generally  referred  to  as  "Student 

Jumni  Relations." 

The  big  question  both  students  and  alumni  might  be  asking 
Why  should  we  care?"  But  for  just  a  minute  let's  think  of 
school  as  a  manufacturer,  the  students  as  raw  materials, 
the  alumni  as  the  finished  products.  As  in  business,  all 
:e  elements  must  be  of  high  quality  and  must  interact  with 
h  other  to  be  successful.  Returning,  then,  to  the  school, 
v  can  students,  alumni,  faculty,  and  administration  best 
:ract  for  maximum  mutual  benefit? 

Alumni  help  to  determine  school  policy,  as  alumni  term 
mbers  of  the  Board  of  Trustees.  The  Alumni  Association 
.  all  alumni  give  both  financial  and  non-financial  support  to 
I,  without  which  it  would  be  extremely  difficult  for  the 
lege  to  operate. 

Alumni,  in  turn,  benefit  through  good  fellowship  and, 
;n  needed,  second  careers  placement.  Alumni  can  be 
rumental  in  organizing  projects  with  their  companies  for 
dents  on  the  WPI  Plan.  They  can  also  give  guidance  to 
-hunting  seniors. 

Several  new  programs  to  further  student  —  alumni  re- 
ons  are  in  either  the  planning  or  implementing  stages.  A 
)klet  is  being  prepared  for  undergraduates  which  will 
cribe  the  basic  purpose,  organization  and  function  of  the 


Alumni  Association.  This  will  help  to  make  the  undergraduates 
more  knowledgeable  and  perhaps  better  alumni.  An  alumni 
counseling  program  is  in  the  organizational  stages  and  will  list 
those  alumni  who  are  willing  to  give  help  to  students  in 
particular  areas,  whether  academic  or  non-academic.  For 
example,  one  category  might  be  student  activities.  There 
would  be  a  list  of  alumni  who  were  active  as  students  in 
campus  activities  and  who  could  be  consulted  by  students 
currently  involved.  Another  category  might  be  career  coun- 
seling in,  say,  sales  engineering  etc.  A  student  who  aspires  to  a 
career  in  sales  could  ask  an  alumnus  active  in  the  field  what 
life  is  really  like.  To  build  these  files,  we  will  need  alumni  who 
are  willing  to  be  contacted  by  students.  Another  program  is  an 
already  existing  alumni  column  in  the  Tech  News  where 
alumni  can  write  articles,  students  can  interview  alumni,  and 
articles  can  be  published  informing  students  of  current 
happenings  with  the  Alumni  Association.  These  are  but  a  few 
of  the  programs  now  under  consideration,  implementation,  or 
operation.  But  we  need  more. 

Ideas  and  programs  are  fine  but  support  and  action  are 
what  is  really  needed  to  further  student  —  alumni  relations.  In 
order  to  develop  good  future  alumni,  students  must  be 
informed  while  still  on  campus  as  to  the  concepts  of  the 
Alumni  Association.  Much  interaction  is  needed  between 
alumni  and  students  to  bridge  any  possible  (oh,  the  old 
expression!)  generation  gap.  The  more  communication,  the 
better  the  understanding,  and  it  will  hopefully  benefit  the 
entire  WPI  community. 

Leonard  Polizzotto  '70 

Chairman,  Student-Alumni  Relations  Committee 

WPI  Alumni  Association 


by  Kenneth  E.  Scott,  '48 
and  Russell  Kay 


Courses  and  classwork  just  ain't  what  they  used  to  be.  To 
be  sure,  there  are  still  lectures  in  big  halls,  and 
laboratories  too,  but  a  revolution  is  sweeping  the  classrooms  at 
WPI. 

A  Carnegie  Corporation  study  called  it  "The  Fourth 
Revolution:  Instructional  Technology  in  Higher  Education." 
The  Simmons  Review  put  it  better:  "The  lamp  of  learning  is 
wired  for  sight  and  sound." 

In  the  past  three  years  at  WPI,  instructional  procedures 
have  undergone  some  drastic  modifications  and  alterations, 
starting  from  the  traditional  classroom  lecture  and  fixed 
laboratory  programs  and  adding  in  increasing  amounts  of 
modern  technology. 

It    may    seem    a    bit    strange    that    a    college   geared    to 
education  for  technology  should  wait  until  the  1970s  to  really 
begin  education  using  technology.  This  timing  is  probably  due 
to  two  factors.  First  was  the  renewal 
of  the  college  with  the  adoption  and 
implementation  of  the  WPI  Plan.  Re- 
thinking the  basic  structure  of  educa- 
tion for  a  technologically  based  world 
inevitably   led   to   a   reexamination   of 
teaching     methods     and     procedures. 

New  demands  on  faculty  and  students  alike  led  to  a  search  for 
ever  more  efficient  ways  of  using  available  time,  people,  and 
other  resources. 

The  second  reason  that  new  instructional  technology  hit  so 
hard  at  this  time  was  that  the  technology  itself  for  effecting 
the  changes  was  ready  —  it  was  available,  affordable,  and  its 
possibilities  were  just  beginning  to  be  realized  across  the 
country. 


Where  Have  All  the  Slide  Rules  Gone? 

It  used  to  be  a  campus  cliche,  especially  at  large  universities, 
that  you  could  always  tell  an  engineering  student  by  the 
leather  slide-rule  holster  dangling  from  his  belt.  While  that  is 
probably  still  true  right  now,  in  a  few  years  the  slide  rule  may 
become  a  collectors'  item.  The  reason,  of  course,  is  the 
pocket-sized  electronic  calculator.  It  is  taster,  more  accurate, 
easier  to  use,  it  remembers  decimal  points  —  and  it  costs  more. 
But  not  all  that  much  more,  and  the  prices  keep  coming  down. 
Mechanical  Engineering  Department  Head  Donald  Zwiep  is 
working  on  a  program  (still  in  the  planning  stages)  that  would 
make  one  of  these  "electronic  slide  rules"  available  to  each 
entering  student  (beginning  next  fall)  for  an  annual  fee  "l 
perhaps  $25  -  and  on  graduation  the  total  rental  the  student 


has  paid  will  be  applicable  to  the  purchase  of  the  instru  I 
Right  now,  calculators  are  available  for  general  use  aj[ 
Gordon  Library,  and  WPI  Bookstore  manager  Harry  Tl  1 
son  says  he  plans  to  carry  calculators  in  stock  immediatel 

The  Coming  of  the  Computer 


Someone   recently   made   the   statement   that   in  five  ye 
knowledge  of  computer  programming  would  be  as  basic  at 
to  the  average  student  as  typing  is  today.  A  lot  of  publicit  I 
been  given  in  the  last  year  to  Dartmouth  College's  large  t 
computer   center  and   the   fact  that   it   is  widely  used  bla 
facets  of  their  student  body.  Well,  perhaps  that  is  unusu; 
Dartmouth.  At  WPI  there  have  been  time-sharing  terminal 
computer    time    available    to    students    since    1969,    at 
student's  ability  to  use  the  computer  is  taken  for  granted. 


LEARNING 


Those  original  time-sharing  capabilities  were  embodii  I 
the    RCA    (now    Univac)    Spectra    70/46,    which    was 
primarily    for   batch    processing.    At    that   time   the   avai 
terminals  were  located  in  WACCC  (the  Worcester  Area  Co 
Computation   Center)   itself.   Temporary   hookups  were 
made    that    allowed    terminals    to    be    placed    in    Salis 
Laboratories    and    Kaven    Hall,    but    these   have   since 
superseded    by    a    permanent    underground    communical 
network  installed  last  summer.

writing  there  are  sixteen  hardwired  computer  terminals  a  i

is  open  twenty-four  hours  a  day.  In  addition  to  these,  thenfl 
many  other  terminals  on  campus  which  tie  in  by  acousil 
coupler  and  telephone.  The  DEC  10  has  a  capability* 
handling  48  terminals,  and  to  make  better  use  of  the  comp  | 
several  other  area  colleges  are  also  tied  in.

eo,  Stage  One 


single  biggest  factor  in  the  learning  revolution  at  WPI  has 
television  —  videotaped,  closed-circuit,  cable-connected, 
Mup-access.  Video  on  campus  is  used  extremely  widely  — 
niklfor  entertainment,   not   for  training  future  broadcasters.

jeach 
low  to 
earn 


for  solid  instruction  in  just  about  every  field  of  study  WPI 
■rs. 

The  beginnings  of  television  at  WPI  were  told  in  the  spring 

1   WPI  Journal,  in  "The  Education  of  the  Idiot  Box."  At 

lit  time  there  was  a  rudimentary  video  studio,  and  a  few 

ulty  members  were  exploring  the  possible  pedagogical  uses 

the   medium.   There   was   one  viewing  station  available  to 

dents  in  the  library. 

Television  use  and  Individually  Prescribed  Instruction  (IPI, 
box)  developed  side  by  side  here.  In  order  to  be  successful, 

;reat  deal  of  planning   and   preparation   must  go  into  the 
''elopment  of  an  IPI  course,  and  this  normally  includes  the 

paration  of  videotapes.  Dr.  Harit  Majmudar  of  the  electrical 


engineering  department  pioneered  the  use  of  videotapes  to 
support  work  in  a  power  engineering  course.  Although  it  was 
not  considered  an  IPI  course  at  the  time,  it  had  many  of  the 
earmarks  of  the  IPI  approach. 

One  of  the  earliest  IPI  courses,  one  which  brought  about 
perhaps  the  biggest  changes,  was  in  the  area  of  control 
engineering.  For  several  years  various  faculty  members  had 
considered  giving  a  single,  cross-discipline  course  sequence  in 
control  engineering,  but  this  never  came  to  pass  for  the  usual 
variety  of  reasons.  With  the  emergence  of  IPI,  however,  and 
with  the  incentive  for  innovation  which  the  WPI  Plan  created, 
the  usual  objections  were  now  overturned  and  a  cross- 
disciplinary  IPI  course  was  instituted. 

This  course  placed  heavy  reliance  on  multimedia  instruc- 
tional aids.  The  most  useful  and  valuable  instructional  tool 
proved  to  be  videotape.  Although  some  tapes  were  prepared  in 
advance  to  present  certain  types  of  material,  the  instructors  of 
the  course  found  an  unexpected  student  demand  for  many 
more  short  videotapes.  Students  wanted  tapes  to  clarify 
points,  explain  principles,  or  just  to  detail  a  shortcut  approach. 
Because  of  this,  two  distinct  types  of  viewing  stations  were 
needed.  There  were  those  in  the  library  for  general  use,  and 
there  were  specific  stations  in  the  laboratory,  adjacent  to  such 
equipment  as  the  analog  computer  and  servo  kits. 


Video,  Stage  Two 

During  that  spring  1971  semester,  there  was  one  viewing 
station  in  the  library.  Only  a  few  courses  were  being  offered 
using  IPI,  and  these  involved  perhaps  200  students.  At  that 
time  the  use  of  video  was  still  considered  experimental. 

More  courses  were  planned  for  IPI,  and  during  the  summer 
of  1971  many  more  videotapes  were  produced.  In  May  1971, 
Professor  Kenneth  Scott  of  the  mechanical  engineering  depart- 
ment was  appointed  half-time  to  administer  and  direct  the 
Institute's  development  of  audiovisual  resources.  In  July  of 
that  year  Herman  Dumas,  an  electronics  technician,  was  hired 
to  supervise  the  studio,  maintain  the  equipment,  and  assist  the 
audiovisual  program  in  general.  The  studio  in  Higgins  Lab  was 
updated,  and  significant  new  equipment  was  added.  (By  the 
way,  there  is  no  truth  to  the  rumor  that  Scott  and  Dumas  are 
referred  to  now  as  Captain  Video  and  the  Video  Ranger.  .  .  .  ) 

A  small  room  in  the  library  was  prepared  to  accommodate 
viewing  stations;  by  spring  1972  five  of  these  were  in  constant 
use  and  the  roorrTwas  overcrowded.  Over  last  summer  a  much 
larger  learning  center  was  created  in  the  library,  capable  of 
holding   up   to   fifteen  viewing  stations.  Right  now  there  are 


The  IPI  Revolution 

In  spring  of  1971  several  courses  were  first  offered  in  a  new 
format,  called  Individually  Prescribed  Instruction  (IPI). 

The  IPI  method  represents  a  complete  change  from  the 
usual  lecture-instructor-classroom-centered  instruction  to  a 
student-centered,  self-paced  learning  format.  Each  student 
works  at  his  own  pace,  mastering  the  course  material  in  a  pre- 
arranged sequence,  one  unit  at  a  time.  The  only  requirement 
is  that  he  must  demonstrate  his  ability  in  one  unit  before  he  is 
allowed  to  move  on  to  the  next  unit  and  more  advanced  ma- 
terial. When  the  student  feels  ready,  he  comes  to  a  learning 
laboratory  where  he  takes  an  assessment  (once  upon  a  time 
it  would  have  been  called  a  test)  on  that  unit.  The  assessment 
is  immediately  corrected  in  a  one-to-one  student-instructor 
relationship.  If  the  student  demonstrates  mastery  of  the  ma- 
terial, he  is  passed  and  immediately  goes  on  to  the  next  unit 
of  instruction.  If  not,  he  goes  back  over  the  same  unit  until  he 
masters  it  and  passes  a  different  assessment.  The  beauty  of 
the  system  is  that  a  student  is  never  allowed  to  get  in  over  his 
head.  Student-to-student  tutoring  is  an  important  part  of  the 
IPI  program. 

Each  IPI  study  unit  contains  an  introduction,  a  well-defined 
set  of  objectives,  a  list  of  instructional  aids,  illustrative  prob- 
lems, study  problems  with  answers,  and  the  types  of  response 
expected  of  the  student.  The  instructional  aids  may  include  a 
primary  course  textbook,  reference  texts,  programmed-learn- 
ing  manuals,  videotapes,  audio  tapes,  and/or  computer  pro- 
grams. The  student  has  a  high  degree  of  freedom  with  respect 
to  choosing  how,  what,  where,  and  when  he  studies.  The 
responsibility  for  learning  is  where  it  must  be,  squarely  oh  the 
shoulders  of  the  student  himself. 

But  what  about  the  students'  point  of  view?  There  are  con- 
flicting feelings,  and  a  small  minority  of  students  seem  unable 
to  adjust  successfully  to  the  kind  of  self-discipline  required  in 
an  IPI  course.  Most  students,  though,  are  in  favor  of  the 
system. 

"IPI  changes  the  student  from  a  passive  to  an  active  learner. 
It  is  relatively  easy  to  sit  in  class  and  listen  to  three  lectures  a 
week.  In  IPI  you  have  to  make  an  active  commitment  to  the 
material  if  you're  going  to  get  anywhere.  You  can't  just  barely 
pass — you  have  to  master  each  increment  of  the  material.  You 
build  up  confidence  with  the  material  because  you  learn  on 
your  own.  You  also  get  more  out  of  the  instructor  because  you 
are  working  on  a  one-to-one  basis.  You  get  to  use  the  faculty 
member  more  to  your  advantage.  IPI  is  more  in  line  with  the 
way  things  are  in  the  real  world.  You  take  on  a  real  project  and 
know  what's  expected  of  you  at  the  end  of  the  term.  It  takes 
initiative,  but  you  progress  at  your  own  rate."  That's  what  Ed 
Peczynski,  '73,  had  to  say  after  taking  his  first  two  IPI  courses 
last  year  and  tutoring  one  other.  He  continues: 

"Tutoring  a  course  helps  the  tutor  as  much  as  the  tutee. 
You  have  to  really  know  and  master  the  material  to  tutor.  You 
get  to  see  things  from  a  different  point  of  view  than  when  you 
first  studied  the  material  and  thought  you  had  mastered  it. 
Students  seem  more  responsive  to  their  peers  as  tutors  rather 
than  faculty  members." 

"You  get  lots  of  confidence  out  of  learning  on  your  own. 
You  learn  a  lot  about  yourself  and  about  how  to  attack  the 
course.  I  will  take  as  many  IPI  courses  as  are  available.  Not 
every  course  should  be  taught  this  way,  but  it  provides  a  good 
balance  in  your  program.  It  makes  for  a  more  varied  and 
interesting  atmosphere  to  combine  various  methods  of 
learning." 


eleven  stations  in  operation  in  that  room.  The  center  is  c] 
fifteen  hours  a  day,  but  even  so  there  are  not  enough  vie\ 
stations  to  satisfy  needs  in  the  immediate  future.  This  I 
approximately  twenty  courses  were  taught  in  the  IPI  fori 
involving  some  3,000  students.  At  present  over  400  videoti 
are  available  in  the  library  learning  center  for  student 
During  Term  A  this  year,  an  average  of  97  students  viewe 
least  one  videotape  every  day  (including  Sundays)  at 
learning  center. 

Videotapes  are  also  being  used  in  many  traditional  for 
courses.  Some  faculty  members  videotape  solutions  to  ho 
work  problems;  others  tape  answers  to  examinations, 
make  these  tapes  available  immediately  after  the  exam, 
advent  of  seven-week  terms  at  WPI  has  meant  the  comp 
revision  of  many  courses,  and  the  problems  of  adapting  to 
new  timetable  have  been  many.  One  beneficial  result  of 
has  been  the  determined  search  for  more  efficient  ways  to 
available  time.  Video  has  been  one  significant  means 
increasing  efficiency  and  learning. 


Other  Media.  .  . 

Video  is  not  the  only  audiovisual  aid  being  used.  One  faci 
member  is  employing  audio-tutorial  methods  to  teach 
laboratory  course  in  sanitary  engineering.  He  prepares  au 
cassette  tapes  which  are  used  to  direct  students  perforrr 
experiments  in  the  laboratory.  Other  faculty  are  using  au 
cassettes  as  a  means  of  giving  directions  to  students  as  t 
begin  laboratory  work.  In  all  cases,  the  use  of  audio-tuto 
methods  has  made  it  possible  for  students  to  come  to 
laboratory  when  they  are  ready  to  perform  the  work. 

Other  faculty  are  experimenting  with  the  use  of  synch 
nized  sight/sound  equipment,  using  2x2  color  slides  ; 
audio  cassettes. 


Video,  Stage  Three:  The  Cable 

The  next  logical  step  in  the  development  of  educational  vie 
is  the  setting  up  of  a  campus-wide  closed-circuit  cable 
system.  During  the  summer  of  11'72  the  WPI  campus  v 
wired  for  learning.  Underground  conduits  and  cables  conni 
all  campus  buildings  to  provide  for  present  and  future  nee 
for  closed-circuit  cable  TV  and  for  hardwired  remote  CO 
puter  terminals. 

Right  now  a  skeleton  cable  TV  system  is  being  instalU 
The    learning    laboratory    in    Higgins    Lab    is    now    using 


'imental  "dial-up"  system:  the  instructor  telephones  the 
udio  and  requests  a  specific  videotape,  which  is  broadcast 
the  studio  to  a  monitor  in  the  learning  lab.  There  is  great 
itial  for  this  type  of  use  of  the  cable;  Oral  Roberts 
rsity  in  Oklahoma  has  an  extensive  computer-organized 
>m-access  video  cable  system  which  works  in  similar 
Dn,  though  on  a  much  larger  and  more  sophisticated  scale, 
here  are  many  other  projected  uses  for  the  cable  system, 
munications  on  campus  need  improving,  and  the  cable 
m  can  enable  the  broadcast  of  general  information, 
uncements,  and  messages.  Video  could  be  transmitted 
any  location  on  campus  to  the  TV  studio  for  taping.  A 
;nt  access  channel  can  be  established,  for  discussion, 
stations,  editorials,  and  entertainment.  (There  exist,  by 
way,  several  vigorous,  student-oriented  "underground" 
orks  based  on  the  use  of  '/2-inch  videotape.  Recent 
rams  from  one  of  these  agencies  have  included  appear-

am  Calley  and  Abbie  Hoffman,  Ralph  Nader  and  Berna-

ns  for  Consenting  Adults."  WPI  does  not  subscribe  to  any

There   are   also   untapped    possibilities   for  interfacing  the 
puter  system  and  the  video  network.
