ng  spring  1972  a  course  entitled  "The  Nuclear  Power 
troversy"  was  videotaped  with  a  live,  participating  audi- 
:  (consisting  mostly  of  high-school  teachers).  The  resulting 
s  were  edited  down  to  eighteen  hours  of  video  and  since 
.  have  been  distributed  widely  as  discussion  material.  As  of 
writing  nearly  forty  other  schools  have  requested  the  use 
:hese  tapes.  The  course  was  sponsored  by  the  Atomic 
rgy   Commission   and   the   Yankee   Atomic   Electric  Com- 


Another  course,  entitled  "Marketing  Management,"  was 
also  videotaped  with  a  live  class.  This  course  will  be  used 
off-campus  as  part  of  WPI's  continuing  education  program. 
Video  now  enables  WPI  to  easily  offer  a  course  at,  for 
example,  an  industrial  plant  in  Pennsylvania.  The  tapes  are 
sent  on  a  weekly  basis,  and  the  WPI  faculty  member  who  is 
teaching  the  course  needs  only  to  make  a  few  trips  to  attend 
the  class  in  person.  This  helps  keep  costs  down  and  it  enables 
the  influence  of  WPI  to  be  spread  much  farther.  Another 
bonus  of  videotape  is  that  the  material  normally  can  be  used 
over  again  with  only  minor  revision  and  updating,  and  this  too 
frees  up  faculty  time  and  helps  increase  the  overall  efficiency 
of  the  instructional  process. 


Labs  and  Learning  Labs 

It's  easy  to  see  how  videotapes  can  replace  lectures.  But 
laboratories? 

Video  has,  in  fact,  proved  to  be  a  significant  addition  to 
the  time-honored  laboratory  approach  to  science  and  engineer- 
ing education.  It  does  not  replace,  but  it  most  certainly  has 
revolutionized  the  process. 

The  lab  experience  started  in  early  1970,  when  enrollment 
in  a  control  engineering  course  increased  to  the  point  where  it 
was  impossible  to  schedule  an  analog  computer  laboratory  in 
the  traditional  manner.  There  just  wasn't  enough  time  and 
enough  computer  capability  under  the  normal  time  restrictions 
of  1:00  to  4:00  on  Tuesday  and  Thursday  afternoons. 

So  the  mechanical  engineering  department  took  a  daring 
step  and  decided  to  allow  students  to  use  the  analog  lab 
whenever  they  wished,  on  a  twenty -four  hour  a  day  basis. 
Video  was  brought  in  as  a  means  of  answering  the  myriad 
questions  that  inevitably  arise  when  a  student  patches  a  board 
and  operates  the  computer  for  the  first  time.  The  normal 
introductory  lectures  and  instruction  on  the  use  of  the  TR-20 
computer  were  still  given,  but  now  students  could  reserve  the 
computer  whenever  they  wished  by  signing  out  a  key  for  the 
laboratory.  By  careful  scheduling  of  assigned  problems,  it  was 
possible  to  accommodate  up  to  100  students  in  the  one  analog 
lab  with  only  one  TR-20  analog  computer. 

A  television  viewing  station  was  set  up  in  the  lab,  and  a 
special  videotape  was  created.  A  student  could  review  the 
whole  tape  before  starting  an  assignment,  or  he  could  dive 
right  in  and  refer  to  particular  sections  of  the  tape  to  answer 
specific  questions  as  they  arose,  because  the  tape  is  indexed  by 
subject.  Even  though  instructors  were  available  during  normal 
hours,   students  were  encouraged   to   use   the  tape  to  obtain 


Technical  Talk 

Videotapes  produced  in  the  WPI  studio  are  recorded  on  1  -inch 
and  Vz-inch  tape  simultaneously.  The  1-inch  tape  provides  a 
good  quality  master  which  is  later  used  to  make  multiple  Vz- 
inch  copies  as  needed  for  student  use.  Usually  a  Vs-inch  copy 
is  kept  as  a  file  master  and  the  1-inch  tape  is  reused.  Until 
recently,  the  only  copy  in  existence  was  the  one  being  used  at 
the  viewing  stations.  This  was  done  in  order  to  minimize  costs. 

The  standard  WPI  viewing  station  consists  of  a  Sony  AV- 
3600  videocorder  and  a  18-inch  monitor.  These  videocorders 
are  of  the  reel-to-reel  type  and  have  proven  to  be  very  re- 
liable in  use;  maintenance  costs  have  been  quite  low.  Video- 
cassette  systems  have  been  explored,  but  the  varying  lengths 
of  current  productions  and  the  need  for  new  playback  equip- 
ment have  so  far  mitigated  against  the  cassette. 

The  television  system  at  WPI  is  strictly  black-and-white, 
primarily  for  economic  reasons — color  recorders  and  monitors 
are  only  marginally  more  expensive  than  for  b/w,  but  the  cost 
of  decent  quality  color  cameras  is  out  of  sight.  (Worcester 
State  College,  however,  has  recently  opened  a  color  studio  on 
its  campus.) 

A  viewing  station  costs  approximately  $900,  including 
necessary  wiring.  New  %-inch  videotape  costs  less  than  $20 
for  a  one-hour  reel.  WPI  studio  costs  normally  run  about  $50 
per  hour.  The  total  cost,  therefore,  to  make  an  original  30- 
minute  tape  is  less  than  $40,  riot  including  faculty  preparation 
time.  Duplicate  30-minute  tapes  cost  $15.  Most  faculty 
members  spend  about  the  same  amount  of  time  preparing  for 
a  30-minute  tape  as  they  would  preparing  for  a  normal  lecture. 


answers  to  their  questions.  The  availability  of  the  tapes 
permitted  round-the-clock  operation  of  the  laboratory  seven 
days  a  week.  This  simultaneously  increased  the  operating 
efficiency  of  the  computer  and  improved  the  quality  of  the 
learning  experience,  because  each  student  was  able  to  spend  as 
much  time  as  he  wanted  or  needed  learning  to  program  and 
operate  the  analog  computer. 

This  first  experiment  in  using  video  aids  for  laboratories 
was  immensely  successful.  It  was  also  favorably  regarded  by 
students.  Among  other  things,  the  lending  of  keys  seemed  to 
increase  the  students'  feelings  of  responsibility  in  using  the 
equipment  and  helped  to  reduce  maintenance  costs.  This  was 
the  first  "hands-on"  use  of  videotape  equipment  by  students, 
and  it  quickly  spread  to  other  uses  in  other  labs. 

Professor  Leslie  C.  Wilbur,  who  teaches  a  basic  laboratory 
course  in  nuclear  engineering,  normally  had  to  spend  many 
hours  detailing  the  use  of  the  radiation  analyzer  and  other 
sophisticated  radiation-detection  devices,  either  to  small 
groups  of  students  or,  often,  to  individuals.  He  put  his 
instructions  on  videotape,  and  a  viewing  station  was  placed  in 
the  nuclear  laboratory  near  the  instruments.  After  this, 
Professor  Wilbur  was  freed  from  the  repetitive  drudgery  and 
had  more  time  to  devote  to  personal  contact  with  his  students. 


The  Continuous  Lab 

Recently  the  life  sciences  department  instituted  a  whole  new 
concept  in  laboratory  procedure,  the  continuous  laboratory,  in 
a  move  designed  to  optimize  student  learning  efficiency  ill  i 
laboratory    area    which    was    limited    both    in    space    and    in 


equipment.  For  courses  in  "Biological  Science"  and  "Gei 
Physiology,"  a  variety  of  laboratory  exercises  were  requi 
but  there  was  only  one  room  with  limited  facilities  availabl 
video  viewing  station  was  established  and  a  number  of  \ 
made,  and  these  were  relied  on  heavily  to  explain 
objectives  of  each  laboratory  exercise,  detail  the  operatio: 
the  equipment  involved,  and  in  some  cases  show  the  re: 
expected.  This  open  laboratory  now  has  different  stul 
working  simultaneously  on  many  different  exercises,  wl 
consequent  savings  in  the  amount  of  equipment  req 
(elimination  of  duplicate  setups)  and,  best  of  all,  no  deci 
in  learning. 

As  an  example  of  the  continuous  biology  lab  in  opera' 
one  exercise  has  students  view  a  videotape  detailing] 
dissection  of  the  frog  sciatic  nerve  and  gastrocnemius  mil 
while  they  perform  their  own  dissections.  The  tape  also  sh 
the  students  how  to  operate  the  stimulator  and  oscillosc 
used  for  the  display  of  nerve  action  potentials. 

Before  the  video-aided  continuous  lab,  the  instructors 
to  spend  inordinate  amounts  of  time  helping  students  \ 
routine  operations  and  problems.  Now  they  are  availabli 
respond  to  more  specialized  and  individualized  questions  f 
students.  Another  benefit  is  that  students  need  to  spend 
time  with  difficult  experimental  procedures  and  are  abli 
move  more  rapidly  through  each  experiment. 


Project  Applications 

Increasing  importance  is  being  given  to  project  work 
students  at  WPI.  One  aspect  of  project  administration  thai 
emerged  is  the  need  of  students  to  acquire  certain  skill 
areas  of  knowledge  quickly,  particularly  with  regard 
measurements  and  instrumentation.  To  help  out  student 
this  situation,  Professor  Kenneth  Scott  has  inaugur 
PIMELL,  a  shorter  way  of  saying  Project  Instrumental  ion 
Measurement  Experimental  Learning  Laboratory. 

The  first  of  the  PIMELL  modular  setups  deals  with 
measurement  of  humidity.  Prepared  programmed  male 
guide  the  student  in  the  use  of  such  instruments  as  an  A 
Dewpointer,  a  sling  psychrometer,  and  a  hygrometer,  while 
use  and  handling  of  the  devices  arc  graphically  illustratec 
videotape.  Once  the  student  has  become  familiar  with 
methods  available  to  him  to  measure  humidity,  he  may  ch< 
the  method  appropriate  to  his  needs  and  sign  out 
instrument  for  project  activity.  Other  PIMELL  module] 
being  established  or  the  measurement  of  temperature,  p 
sure,  flow,  and  so  forth.

Responsibility  From  the  Bottom  Up 


The  overriding  trend  in  educational  practice  today  is  toward 
more  freedom:  freedom  in  picking  the  method,  the  time,  the 
place  of  learning.  To  be  successful,  this  freedom  must  be 
accompanied  by  an  increase  in  individual  student  and  faculty 
responsibility.  The  heavy  use  of  television  and  other  advanced 
technological  aids  at  WP1  has  helped  provide  students  with  this 
new  freedom,  and  it  has  given  them  an  opportunity  to 
demonstrate  increased  responsibility.  As  the  focus  of  activity 
leans  more  strongly  to  project  work  and  the  open  laboratory 
format,  learning  now  becomes  the  constant  factor  and  time 
and  method  are  variables. 

An  important  factor  in  the  success  of  the  new  methods  is 
the  support  they  have  received  from  everyone  —  students, 
faculty,  and  administration.  At  some  colleges,  large  and 
expensive  television  systems  have  been  installed,  but  they  are 
scarcely  used.  The  reason  is  that  only  a  few  people  saw  —  or 
see  —  any  worth  in  those  systems.  There,  considerations  and 
opinions  of  students  and  faculty  were  not  an  integral  part  of 
the  planning. 

At  WP1  everything  has  grown  from  the  bottom  up.  Faculty 
saw  a  need,  initiated  the  use,  created  the  demand;  only  then 
was  significant  investment  made  in  the  systems  currently  being 
used.  Faculty  have  found  new  outlets  for  creative  teaching 
efforts;  students  have  readily  accepted  the  new  approaches;  all 
over  campus  the  interest  and  excitement  of  learning  are 
accelerating. 

Technology,  at  last,  is  beginning  to  pay  back  some  of  its 
debt  to  the  educational  process  that  has  nurtured  it  for  so 
long.  i — I 


Ia  I  e  are  l°°king  over  a  second  grader's  shoulder  d 
|r  \|  reading  and  spelling  lesson.  The  year  is  1980.  T 
age  7,  is  seated  before  a  20-inch  color  television 
equipped  with  a  typewriter  keyboard  and  a  lightpen  (sijei 
like  a  small  flashlight  that  can  be  pointed  at  the  sag] 
indicate  an  answer).  His  instructor's  face  appears 
screen,  cordially  greets  him  and  asks  his  name.  After  1m 
types  it  out,  the  instructor  says:  "Well,  Tommy,  are  yoi 
to  begin?"  The  instructor  now  asks  him  to  spell 
Tommy  slowly  types  the  letters,  which  instantly  appear 
screen.  If  he  is  correct,  he  is  praised  and  then  promjsd 
spell  a  harder  word,  and  so  the  lesson  continues.  If  In 
was  wrong,  the  instructor  will  attempt  to  help  Tomjy 
where  he  went  wrong.

During  this  entire  lesson,  Tommy  will  be  the  only 
involved.  The  "instructor"  is  a  computer,  which  will  g. 
the  face  on  the  screen,  supply  the  voice,  and  guide  the 
throughout.  The  results  of  the  entire  episode  will  be 
out  later  for  Tommy's  "real"  teacher  (a  live  human  be 
examine  and  use  in  gauging  Tommy's  progress,  to  po 
those  areas  where  Tommy  may  need  extra  help  or  drill. 

What  makes  this  a  lesson  in  1980  rather  than  one 
advanced  classroom  today  is  the  face  on  the  screen.  It  i 
videotaped  picture,  for  that  could  not  be  readily  adap 
different  responses  from  Tommy.  Instead  it  is  an  an 
face  generated  by  the  computer  itself.  The  use  of  a  fi 
face  (and  other  types  of  animation)  will,  naturally, 
Tommy  much  more  excited  and  eager  to  interact  w 
mechanical  instructor  —  a  big  step  over  a  simple  prere> 
audio  message. 

The  Face

Ii 


Stephen  R.  Alpert,  "67,  is  assistant 

professor  of  computer  si  ience  Jl  WPl. 


The  face  on  the  screen  is  one  of  the  more  exciting  aspc 
the  field  known  as  computer  graphics.  Ever  since  man  >|rl 
to  scratch  marks  onto  the  wall  of  a  cave,  he  has  con* 
searched  for  more  advanced  tools  to  aid  him  in  v 
expressing  his  ideas.  In  recent  years,  computers  have  b< 
an  important  aid  to  many  artists.  In  fact,  the  n' 
computer  and  its  graphic  display  possibilities  may  I 
artist's  most  interesting  tool  since  the  advent  ot  oil  paint 
What  problems  must  be  overcome  to  produce  con 
animation  of  the  sort  mentioned  earlier?  The  first  prob 
that  of  storing  the  face  in  digital  form  in  the  com] 
memory.  At  this  stage  of  the  art,  at  least,  a  basic  compi 
is  made.    The  face  is  considered  as  .1  sencs  of  polygons,  u 


10 


a  maximum  of  six  sides,  attached  together  just  like  a 
er's  web.  Such  a  face  is  depicted  on  the  cover  of  this  issue. 
So  far,  things  are  relatively  simple.  However,  when  one  or 
e  points  move,  for  instance  when  an  eye  blinks,  these 
ions  exert  forces  on  the  entire  network  of  lines  and  the 
must  distort  in  the  proper  direction  and  to  the  degree  that 
uman  face  does.  For  each  line  in  the  figure,  many 
illations  must  be  made  to  determine  in  which  direction 
connecting  line  moves  and  how  far  it  stretches, 
sider:  the  lines  toward  the  middle  of  the  cheek  must  be 
y  elastic,  while  those  on  the  forehead  are  much  more  rigid, 
computer  has  to  take  all  this  into  account  if  the  animation 
be  realistic.  Many  millions  of  calculations  and  a  second  or 
ter,  the  face  appears  on  the  screen  transformed  to  its  new 
:ion. 

The  spider's  web  of  lines  that  we  have  now  does  not  look 

a  face,  so  we  next  add  hair,  eyes,  eyebrows,  a  mouth,  and 

At  the  same  time  we  add  a  source  of  light  in  front  of  the 

and  shade  the  polygons  according  to  their  angle  relative  to 

light   source   (Figure   1).  We  now  have  a  face  that  looks 

e-dimensional,  but  it  has  the  appearance  of  being  roughly 

|-ed  from  wood.  So  we  apply  some  computerized  sandpaper 

hading  algorithm)  to  smooth  off  the  sharp  ridges,  and  the 

lit  is  a  face  with  a  much  more  lifelike  appearance  (Figure 

All  of  these  calculations  must  be  done  at  each  step  in  order 
create  animation.  In  practical  terms,  this  means  that  most 
iputer-generated  "movies"  are  filmed  a  frame  at  a  time  and 
In  speeded  up  to  simulate  the  actual  movement.  Because  of 
I  time  scale  involved,  only  the  simplest  of  pictures  can  be 
Ived  directly  off  a  computer  display  screen  and  still  give  a 
Bse  of  life.  But  with  increasing  computer  speeds  and  program 

■  histication,  this  problem  will  be  eliminated  over  the  next 
r  years. 

i  Anyway,  now  that  we  have  generated  a  face  that  moves, 

■  "simply"  add  a  voice  to  the  face  and  the  computer  will  talk 
lus,  eye  to  eye!  Computer  animation  of  this  type  will  serve 
la  prime  tool  in  computer-aided  instruction  for  elementary 
lool  children  in  years  to  come.

Interactive  Graphics 

Computer  graphics  have  applications  for  grown-ups,  too. 
Representative  of  the  work  being  done  in  many  places  are  two 
projects  developed  at  Sanders  Associates  in  Nashua,  New 
Hampshire. 

The  first  project  is  typical  of  the  uses  of  interactive 
graphics  systems  in  the  command  and  control  situation.  Figure 
3  shows  the  geographic  boundaries  of  the  various  divisions  of 
the  Los  Angeles  Police  Department.  A  dispatcher  at  a  display 
console  can  select  any  given  division,  and  then  one-mile-square 


11 


Figure  3 


Figure  2 


subdivisions  for  closer  inspection.  Figure  4  illustrates  a 
detailed  street  map  and  incident-reporting  format  as  an 
emergency  vehicle  dispatcher  would  see  it.  Three  different 
types  of  vehicles  have  been  highlighted  to  show  their  current 
location.  The  function  boxes  at  the  bottom  of  the  screen  are 
quick-action  buttons  that  will  perform  a  predetermined 
operation.  For  example,  if  the  dispatcher  selects  the  DISP 
AMB  box,  an  ambulance  will  be  automatically  dispatched  to 
the  address  reported  in  the  incident-reporting  section  of  the 
screen. 

Another  application  with  many  future  uses  is  as  a  part  of 
computer  simulation.  Figure  5  shows  a  typical  air  traffic 
control  pattern  as  it  would  appear  on  a  radar  screen.  Each  data 
block  on  the  screen  represents  an  aircraft  under  the  direction 


of  a  particular  air  traffic  controller.  History  tracks 
indicated  as  slash  lines;  up  to  six  of  these  history  lines  can  h 
shown  at  once.  The  tabular  information  at  the  right  preset^ 
the  current  situation  in  text  form.  With  the  use  of  this  systel 
air  traffic  controllers  can  be  trained  under  realistic  COnditifl 
without  endangering  the  lives  of  air  passengers.  If  a  mistake 
made  here,  no  real  crashes  and  no  disruption  of  air  traffic  wi 
result. 


13 


liphics  at  WPI 

^rk  is  being  done  at  WPI  in  several  fields  of  study,  notably 
eiputerized  plotting.  Stereographic  projections  can  be  drawn 
fcive  a  three-dimensional  view  of  many  atomic  and  molecu- 


lar structures.  Some  of  the  beauty  and  symmetry  of  mathe- 
matics can  be  seen  in  graphs  generated  by  simple  equations 
(Figure  6),  which  are  computer-drawn  in  a  few  minutes.  A 
person  would  require  several  hours  to  produce  the  same 
picture. 


I 


Figure  7 


Figure  t 


Another  technique  is  called  "interrupted  line"  plotting. 
This  produces  perspective  drawings  of  three-dimensional  sur- 
faces (Figure  7)  with  a  precision,  accuracy,  and  speed  that 
would  make  any  draftsman  jealous  —  and  obsolete. 

At  the  moment,  three  WPI  students  are  attempting  to  use 
the  computer  to  simulate  the  composition  of  music.  This  may 
sound  old-hat,  but  these  students  want  the  computer  to  go 
farther  than  merely  typing  out  the  letters  of  the  notes.  They 
want  the  computer  to  produce  its  composition  in  full  musical 
notation  (Figure  8).  They  have  not  yet  found  a  volunteer  to 
play  their  creations. 

These  are  but  a  few  examples  of  the  exciting  work  being  done 


in  computer  graphics  today.  With  the  cost  of  computer 
and  display  equipment  decreasing  rapidly,  prospects  are  brig 
for  even  more  mind-boggling  uses  of  computer  graphics  in  the 
future. 

Perhaps  you  remember  some  of  the  space-ship  scenes  from 
Stanley  Kubrick's  film,  2001 ,  where  often  dozens  of  displaf 
screens  would  be  in  front  of  the  astronauts,  each  screen  giviA 
a  constantly  changing  animated  display  of  one  sort  ofd.ua  or 
another.  Kubrick  faked  it  for  the  film,  with  16mm  movial 
rear-projected  on  to  each  of  those  screens.  But  the  day  I 
coming  when  that  may  be  the  best  way  to  present  a  pilot  with 
the  split-second  information  he  needs.  The  technology  for  thai 
will  soon  be  a  reality. 


Ihe  Passing  of 
physics  One 


With  the  coming  of  spring  this  year  at  WPI,  one  of  the 
1  common  memories  of  generations  of  WPI  graduates  has 
ej  passed  into  the  realm  of  memory.  General  Physics,  the 
s|  course  which  used  to  be  required  of  all  undergraduates, 
llj.o  longer  be  the  same  because  of  the  departure  of  the  two 
enAfho  watched  over  it  for  those  many  years.  Professor  Kurt 
ftiyer  died  suddenly  on  February  22,  stricken  with  a  heart 
tic  while  talking  with  a  student  in  his  office.  Edwin 
icrson,  the  physics  lecture  assistant  who  set  up  demonstra- 
ji3  and  maintained  lab  equipment  for  44  years,  retired 
;biary  1. 


<JRT  MAYER  was  not  a  man  easily  forgotten.  Walter  B. 
Dennen,  Jr.,  '51,  spoke  with  several  people  about  him 
cscitly.  "When  I  asked  if  they  remembered  him,  everyone 
nd  in  fond  remembrance.  Some  could  not  offer  specific 
;>nnents,  but  they  nevertheless  remembered  enough  to 
ii  ." 

■  layer  was  a  native  of  Germany  who  joined  the  WPI 
ajty  in  1942.  Professor  Allan  Parker,  head  of  the  physics 
:prtment  from  1949  to  1972,  talked  about  Mayer's  relation- 
ijwith  his  students.  "When  a  student  came  to  him  for  help, 
1  ould  give  him  quite  a  lot  of  help.  But  he  wanted  the 
a«:nt  to  learn  to  think  for  himself,  and  Mayer  would 
aaially  draw  out  the  student  so  that  he  would  end  up 
sering  his  own  question. 

jl'He   was    most    concerned    with    teaching,   and   with   the 

ii>sophy  of  physics.  And  though  he  was  a  philosopher,  he 

lia  deep  belief  in  the  experimental  method.  He  was  really 

eiliked    by    his    students,    as    an   advisor   and   a   counselor, 

cgh   in  his  earlier  days  at  WPI   he  was  best  known  as  a 

Jrer,  the  most  colorful  lecturer  at  WPI." 

;  5 ill    Lupoli,    '61,    reminisced    about    Mayer's    classroom 

•;nce.  "Professor  Mayer  would  scan  the  lecture  hall,  smiling 

llie  while,  and  suddenly  call  out  your  name  and  ask  you  a 

ation.  I  always  felt  that  he  was  not  trying  to  involve  us  in 

lectures  as  much  as  he  was  attempting  to  keep  us  alert. 

In  we  entered  his  classroom  or  lecture  hall,  we  would  find 

^blackboards  filled  with  his  outline  and  equations.  If  we 

t  our  time  copying  the  material  rather  than  listening  to 

we  had  to  be  losers  since  his  tests  were  always  based  on 

1  :  he  said.  He  was  a  tough  man  to  keep  pace  with." 

\z  times,  Mayer  did  seem  to  have  a  fearsome  reputation 

ng   the   student  body.  Vince  Pace,  '71,  commented  that 

seemed  to  enjoy  overwhelming  the  freshmen,  keeping  us 


off  balance.  I'll  always  picture  him  entering  the  classroom  with 
that  massive  volume  of  Newton's  Laws  under  his  arm.  I  finally 
concluded  it  was  just  a  stage  prop,  but  it  sure  worked  on  us." 

The  Rev.  Ed  Swanson,  '45,  has  a  colorful  memory  of 
Mayer's  first  year  at  WPI.  "I  remember  vividly  and  with  much 
appreciation  being  in  one  of  Dr.  Mayer's  first  physics  sections. 
I  was  a  freshman  at  WPI,  it  must  have  been  in  the  spring  of 
1942,  and  he  had  only  recently  come  to  this  country.  He 
spoke  with  a  thick  German  accent  which  sometimes  made  us 
smile,  and  his  syntax  left  something  to  be  desired.  On  one 
occasion,  when  he  caught  us  smiling  at  one  of  his  expressions, 
he  said:  'Gentlemen,  please  do  not  laugh  at  me.  I  have  not 
been  in  this  country  yet  a  long  distance. '  That  really  broke  us 
up.  He  was  a  good,  kindly  man,  and  I  remember  him  with 
great  fondness." 

One  of  his  students,  Dr.  Robert  E.  Seamon,  '61,  now  on 
the  staff  of  the  Los  Alamos  Scientific  Laboratory,  held  Mayer 
in  special  regard.  He  summed  up  his  feelings  in  this  manner: 

"Perhaps  the  principal  advantage  to  be  derived  from 
attending  a  small  college  like  WPI  is  the  opportunity  for  close 
contact  with  members  of  the  faculty,  among  them  tremendous 
personalities  like  Kurt  L.  Mayer,  who  holds  a  prized  spot  in 
the  memories  of  many  whose  lives  he  touched.  He  was  an 
unforgettable  person;  no  one  could  have  worked  or  studied 
with  him  without  realizing  his  love  for  physics  and  the  joy  and 
enthusiasm  with  which  he  approached  his  academic  responsi- 
bilities. 


Prof.  Mayer  at  his  physics  lecture — from, 
endpapers  of  the  1960  Peddler 


"Professor  Mayer  was  the  best  teacher  under  whom  I  ever 
studied  —  a  master  from  the  viewpoint  of  his  understanding  of 
good  pedagogy.  His  thoroughness  as  an  instructor  provided  me 
with  a  good  foundation  in  classical  physics  which  was  most 
important  to  me  in  graduate  school.  I  believe  he  was  most 
successful  as  a  teacher  because  of  his  realization  that  one 
learns  best  through  practice  and  repetition.  He  would  give  us 
problem  set  after  problem  set,  most  of  which  he  graded 
himself.  How  often  that  little  note  —  'See  me!  KLM' 
appeared  on  his  students'  papers  —  just  one  measure  of  the 
hours  of  time  he  was  willing  to  spend  in  personal  conference 
with  them.  Those  who  took  advantage  of  all  the  extra  effort 
he  put  forth  were  undoubtedly  the  beneficiaries. 

"We  can  remember  him  for  many  things:  his  immaculate 
appearance,  his  sharp  dress,  his  use  of  practically  every 
conceivable  demonstration  in  P-3  lectures,  his  mechanics  notes 
with  which  he  would  fill  the  blackboards  on  three  walls,  his 
pride  in  having  walked  to  the  Institute  and  arrived  promptly 
on  the  snowiest  of  mornings;  but  most  of  all  his  concern  for 
the  physical,  emotional,  and  educational  well-being  of  his 
students.  Those  who  kept  in  contact  after  they  left  the 
Institute  realize  how  pleased  he  was  to  hear  from  his  former 
students,  his  pleasure  in  seeing  their  names  among  (he  authors 
of  scholarly  and  technical  articles,  and  his  joy  in  receiving 
reprints  of  their  work.    He   always  would  inquire  about  my 


classmates  by  name  and  be  disappointed  if  I  had  nothing  m 
to  report. 

"As  far  as  I  am  aware,  his  greatest  professional  concern 
recent  years  was  to  maintain  Tech's  reputation  as  an  01 
standing  undergraduate  institution  rather  than  to  eomjfl 
with  the  large  universities  in  graduate  education.  Profess 
Mayer  was  convinced  that  the  finest  contribution  WPI  coo 
make  to  the  scientific  and  engineering  communities  was 
produce  college  graduates  who  could  be  counted  on  to  do  Ml 
in  their  careers  -  to  stand  among  the  best. 

"Often  we  fail  to  tell  others  how  much  they  have  meant) 
us  until  suddenly  it  is  too  late.  I  hope  Professor  Mayer  realij] 
that  he  had  a  profound  influence  on  my  life,  for  in  addition] 
all  that  he  taught  me  he  urged  me  into  graduate  work  ai 
helped  me  find  an  acceptable  and  realistic  accommodation! 
interests  in  physics  and  music.  Besides  being  grateful,  I  a 
proud  that  I  feel  able  to  acknowledge  him  as  having  been  n 
friend." 

Kurt  Mayer's  passing  was  deeply  felt  on  campus.  A  meeting 
faculty  and  students  who  knew  him  decided  that  the  best  wi 
to  honor  his  memory,  in  view  ot  Ins  lifelong  dedii 
teaching,  is  to  establish  a  scholarship  fund  in  his  name,  Alum 
who  wish  to  contribute  to  the  fund  may  do  so  in  care  of  tl 
University  Relations  Office  ai  WPI.

the   aisle  of  electricity,  Edwin  Anderson  rubbed  a  thick

I  <  of  his  hand.  "Static  electricity,"  he  proclaimed,  as  the 
i  s  on  the  back  of  his  hand  reached  up  for  the  rod. 
I  As  a  physics  lecture  assistant,  Edwin  Anderson  has  spent 
|  ^ears  setting  up  experiments  for  WPI  students  to  prove  the 
tence  of,  and  to  measure,  light  and  sound,  heat  and  cold,

He   pointed   out   instruments   as  though   introducing   old, 
^ble  friends.  Many  are  no  longer  used,  victims  of  progress.

a  down  on  lectures. 

But  Anderson  remembers  years  of  busier  days  when  his 
;ieriments  regularly  proved  basic  physics  principles.  "These 
Bigs,"  he  gave  a  sweeping  gesture  to  the  shelves  crammed 
wh  instruments,  "were  all  used  for  dozens  of  different 
:»eriments.  The  basics  are  the  same.  Some  of  these  instru- 
flnts  really  haven't  been  improved.  The  basic  principles  of 
i/sics  stay  the  same." 

|  In  the  beginning,  Anderson  intended  the  lecture  assistant 
a  to  be  temporary  and  hoped  to  continue  his  formal 
Sication.  "But  the  depression  came,"  he  said.  "I  know  the 
lie  principles,  but  I  never  got  the  higher  mathematics." 
I  On  a  middle  shelf  in  an  aisle  of  mixed  instruments, 
^jlderson  leafed  through  a  dusty  pile  of  barometric  pressure 
lirts.  He  found  the  one  he  was  looking  for  —  the  day  of  the 
frricane  of  1938.  Next  to  the  charts  were  a  dozen  dusty 
Ifometers.  "We  used  to  have  the  boys  carry  them  up  to 
licroft  Tower  to  measure  the  difference  in  barometric 
Assure  as  you  get  higher." 

I  "I  collect  antiques.  Some  of  these  instruments  are  an- 
lues.  Some  were  old  when  I  came  here."  He  stopped  near  a 
I  old  black  projector  that  took  large  glass  slides.  It  carried  a 
Ite  saying  it  was  donated  by  the  Class  of  1897  in  1928.  On  a 
krby  table  Anderson  pointed  out  the  modern  slide  projector 
Id  today.  The  old  projector  was  gathering  dust. 
!  Anderson  said  he  will  avoid  the  old  projector's  fate.  After 
I  years  of  the  science  of  physics,  Anderson  will  devote  much 
(  his  retirement  time  to  photography.  "I  really  enjoy 
jotography.  So  far,  I've  been  taking  mostly  color  slides." 


He  said  he  will  be  using  some  of  the  skills  acquired  in  his 
work  for  his  own  pleasure.  "You  have  to  be  everything  to  do 
this  job  —  an  audio  man,  a  carpenter,  an  electrician." 

In  the  busier  days,  Anderson  preferred  to  build  rather  than 
buy  equipment  when  possible.  Two  of  his  amplifiers  were 
being  used  in  a  lecture  while  he  was  being  interviewed.  "I  built 
them  15  years  ago,  and  they're  still  working." 

Will  he  miss  the  job? 

"I  suppose  I  will.  I've  enjoyed  working  with  the  kids.  I  call 
them  kids,  but  I  probably  shouldn't.  They're  wonderful 
people." 

"I  told  them  I'd  come  back  if  they  needed  help  with 
anything,  but  not  on  the  first  day  of  retirement.  I  want  to 
sleep  until  9  a.m.  for  a  change."

Introduction  to  Yoga 

The    heat    in    the    windowless  wrestling   room  at   Harringj 
Auditorium  rose  to  a  stifling  80  degrees. 

"The  atmosphere  in  here  is  not  exactly  conducive  to 
practice    of   good    yoga,"    announces   Margaret    Kranich, 
instructor  of  the  course  along  with  her  husband,  Dr.  WilnJ 
Kranich,  the  George  C.  Gordon  Professor  of  Chemical  E( 
neering  and  department  head  at  WPI. 

"But  we'll  manage,"  she  continues.  "Everybody  relax 
the  mats  while  we  listen  to  a  lecture  on  Hinduism  and  Yogi 

Dr.  Kranich  talks  about  the  concept  of  Hinduism,  reino 
nation,  and  the  yoga  philosophy.  He  says  that  Hindus  belis 
that  people  are  reborn  again  and  again  into  either  higher! 
lower  life  forms,  and  that  how  one  lives  his  life  determin 
whether  he  will  have  a  good  or  bad  karma.  (According  to  d 
Hindu  explanation,  any  apparently  undeserved  pleasure  i 
pain  comes  as  a  result  of  deeds  done  in  a  previous  existena 

He  explains  that  priestly  asceticism  has  been  of  prin 
importance  in  India.  Cutting  onself  off  from  the  endless  whi 
of  life,  death,  and  rebirth  in  order  to  be  at  one  with  d 
universal  spirit  becomes  the  be-all  and  end-all  of  a  Hindu  yog 
existence.  Some  yoga  practitioners  are  able  to  meditji 
unceasingly  for  days,  weeks,  even  years  at  a  time. 

Yogi  ascetics  live  alone  and  exercise  complete  control  oil 
their  minds  and  bodies.  They  have  no  valuable  earti 
possessions,  believing  that  desire  is  the  key  to  evil.  In  order| 
meditate  undisturbed  they  find  a  firm  place  to  sit  an 
concentrate.  Assuming  the  cross-legged  lotus  position  wft 
head,  neck,  and  spine  erect  and  vision  indrawn,  a  y0| 
transports  himself  to  ultimate  peace. 

Dr.  Kranich's  talk  is  over.  The  students  who  are  lyit 
down  sit  up  on  the  floor  mats.  The  seated  students  stretch  01 
their  legs  and  shake  them. 

Mrs.  Kranich  tells  the  class  to  stand  up  and  get  ready  fi 
yoga  exercises,  warning  everyone  not  to  push  too  hard.  After 
number  of  stretching  exercises  and  a  series  of  knee-stretchB 
bends,  she  feels  that  the  students  are  ready  for  the  ploiJ 
position. 

The  young  yoga  enthusiasts  lie  on  their  backs  and  pu 
their  feet  up  over  their  heads  with  legs  straight.  About  eight  ( 
the  twenty-five  present  manage  to  touch  their  toes  CO  th 
floor. 

"Not  bad,"  comments  Mrs.  Kranich.  "Now  do  the  fish." 

The  students,  still  on  their  backs,  arch  up  their  chests  M 
rest  the  tops  of  their  heads  on  the  floor. 

"A  good  lung  stretcher,"  observes  the  instructor,  wh 
takes  time  out  to  teach  the  class  yoga  breathing. 

"Don't  breath  COO  deeply  or  too  long,"  she  cautiol 
"There's  a  danger  that  you  can  become  super  oxygenated  ■ 
take  off  on  a  trip  that  you  don't  expect." 

After  the  breathing  exercises  she  has  everyone  writhing! 


'( 


intal  position  mimicking  cobras.  When  the  "cobras"  turn 
•""cats"  the  exercise  period  is  over. 

'erspiration  drips  off  the  participants,  but  no  one  is 
plaining  of  the  heat  or  sore  muscles.  In  fact,  most  feel  in 
ap  condition,  their  limbs  limbered,  mental  cobwebs

Joseph's  Abbey 

iks  are  "holier  than  thou."  Monks  have  no  sense  of  humor. 

Iks  are  an  anachronism  in  this  age  of  men  on  the  moon.

tVrong!     At    least    at    St.    Joseph's    Abbey,    a    Christian 

lastic  commune  in  Spencer,  Mass. 

Brother  Basil  (the  Reverend  M.  Basil  Pennington),  garbed 

i  long  white  robe,  black  tunic,  and  hooded  bench-warmer 

let,  greeted  Prof.  Donald  Johnson's  Intersession  group  in 

It  of  the  abbey  church. 

He  smiled,  even  though  the  students  were  a  few  minutes 

i   (Their  very  large  bus  had  become  unexpectedly  trapped 

de  a  very  small  walled  courtyard,  but  an  expert  bus  driver

"Welcome   to   St.   Joseph's,"   said    Brother  Basil.  "You're

He  ushered  the  group  into  a  semienclosed  section  of  the 

rch  where  the  astonishingly  modern  and  beautiful  guitar

Following  the  service  the  women  were  gently  but  firmly 
segregated  from  the  men. 

"I'm  sorry,"  Brother  Basil  explained,  "but  the  ladies  will 
have  to  go  with  Brother  Raphael  while  the  men  are  shown 
through  the  main  parts  of  the  cloister  and  the  abbey." 

When  asked  why,  he  answered:  "Because  women  aren't 
prospective  monks." 

So  Brother  Raphael  led  his  flock  to  a  charmingly  paneled 
room  where  he  told  them  about  communal  living  as  experi- 
enced at  the  Trappist  community.  He  explained  that  a  monk's 
day  starts  at  3:15  a.m.  and  ends  at  8:00  p.m.  Worship  services, 
private  meditation,  and  prayer  are  interspersed  with  periods  of 
work  and  silence. 

Work  could  be  teaching  scripture,  making  vestments  and 
jams,  or  doing  maintenance  jobs. 

He  smiled.  "Sometimes  we  do  things  that  are  not  quite  up 
to  our  potential,"  he  said.  "For  instance  for  several  years  one 
of  the  brothers,  who  was  a  well-known  artist  outside,  worked 
as  a  bricklayer  when  he  joined  the  order.  We  needed 
bricklayers  more  than  artists  at  the  time." 

Hoping  not  to  give  the  wrong  impression,  he  explained 
further.  "We  usually  do  find  a  niche  where  our  talents  fit  in 
best.  And  we  are  not  poured  into  a  strict  mold.  Religiously 
speaking,  for  example,  we  range  from  the  far  left  to  the  far 
right." 

"Where  do  most  of  the  monks  come  from?"  he  was  asked. 

"From  the  city,"  he  replied.  "The  median  age  of  the 
brothers  is  38  and  we  have  a  waiting  list.  Actually,"  he 
continued,  "a  widower  usually  finds  that  he  can  adapt  more 
readily  to  our  way  of  life.  He  has  already  learned  how  to  live 
with  others." 

Brother  Raphael  went  on  to  say  that  the  brothers  were 
allowed  to  visit  their  homes  if  their  parents  were  ill  and  could 
not  travel  to  visit  them  at  the  abbey.  Sometimes  travel  was 
allowed  for  study  or  to  aid  in  the  construction  of  a  new  abbey. 

It  was  time  to  meet  the  men  for  a  slide  show  which  was  to 
be  presented  by  Brother  Gerald.  Along  the  way  Brother 
Raphael  asked,  "What  do  you  suppose  this  stone  structure  is?" 

"It  looks  like  a  high  well,"  a  girl  replied. 

"It  was  a  windmill  once,"  he  said.  "We  had  a  meeting  and 
everyone  thought  that  it  would  be  a  great  place  to  put  pigeons. 
For  three  days  everyone  thought  that.  Then  somebody 
remembered  what  pigeons  do.  .  .  " 

The  visit  to  St.  Joseph's  was  nearly  over.  But  the  students 
couldn't  leave  without  a  stop  at  the  abbey  gift  shop.  There 
they  stocked  up  on  the  sinfully  rich  jam  and  fudge  whipped  up 
by  the  brothers  during  their  work  periods. 

With  each  purchase  the  monk  at  the  cash  register  gave  a 
gift  in  return.  He  said,  "God  bless  you."

"That  Old  Black  Magic.  .  .  " 

Who  lives  in  a  black  house  in  the  woods,  has  a  tombstone  in 
the  yard,  and  a  skull  inside  the  house? 

If  you  guessed  a  witch,  you'd  be  right.  But  if  you  thought 
it  was  a  lady  witch,  you  were  wrong. 

This  witch  is  blond,  has  a  pierced  ear,  deep  blue  eyes,  and 
his  name  is  Ted  Rabouin.  Although  he's  billed  as  the  Warlock 
of  Westboro  (Mass.),  he  prefers  to  be  called  a  male  witch 
rather  than  a  warlock.  He  admits  to  being  the  head  of  the 
witches'  cult  in  New  England. 

As  one  of  the  speakers  in  TProf.  Thomas  Keil's  witchcraft 
class,  he  fascinated  his  audience  with  tales  of  his  psychic 
abilities,  his  use  of  tarot  cards  in  telling  fortunes,  and  his 
unusual  home  life. 

He  explained  that  electricity  interferes  with  his  natural 
powers  so  he  does  all  his  clairvoyant  work  and  readings  by 
candlelight.  Mostly  women  and  young  people  seek  his  services. 

Rabouin  loves  cats  and  has  a  house  full  of  them. 

"Not  because  I'm  a  witch,"  he  says.  "I  just  like  to  have 
them  around." 

His  real  affection  for  cats  is  one  reason  he  refuses  to 
participate  in  black  magic,  because  this  type  of  witchcraft 
requires  the  sacrificing  of  animals.  He  is,  in  fact,  a  staunch 
supporter  of  the  Antivivisection  Society. 

He  also  has  strong  feelings  about  conveying  his  authentic- 
ity as  a  witch.  Known  nationally,  he  has  appeared  on  the 
David  Frost  television  show  and  has  met  with  a  number  of 
experts  in  psychic  circles. 

Another  local  witch,  Ron  Parshley  of  Worcester  radio 
station  WAAB,  spoke  about  witchcraft  as  a  religion.  He 
believes  that  it  frees  a  person's  mind  and  offers  him  limitless 
opportunity.  Ritual  magic,  he  says,  is  the  manipulation  of 
energy  and  can  be  done  by  anyone.  A  witch,  however,  must  do 
the  more  formal  ceremonies. 

According  to  Parshley,  harmony  is  the  main  purpose  of  the 
practice  of  witchcraft.  It  can  help  heal  an  ill  person  or  boost 
someone  up  the  ladder  of  success.  This  process  is  called  white 
magic  and  is  one  of  the  positive  facets  of  witchcraft. 

"I  suppose  you've  heard  about  the  nudity  and  orgies  that 
take  place  at  our  ceremonies,"  he  said.  "The  only  reason  for 
the  nudity,"  he  explained,  "is  to  help  each  member  of  the 
coven  receive  increased  psychic  energy.  Clothes  get  in  the  way 
by  interrupting  the  energy  process.  I'm  not  saying  that  there  is 


no  sex  at  our  meetings.  After  all  witchcraft  is  based  on  8 
concept  of  fertility.  But  when  there  is  sex,  it  is  responsib 

He  laughed.  "If  you  still  have  inhibitions,  you  can  alt 
join  a  cult  that  wears  bathing  suits  during  the  ceremoi 
That's  the  thing  about  wicca  (witchcraft).  You  can  do  aln 
anything  you  want." 

Mrs.  Enid  Hoffman,  from  the  University  of  Connecti 
told  a  remarkable  story  of  how  she  exorcised  a  bad  spirit  fi 
a  bookcase  wall  in  a  student's  apartment.  The  ghost  had 
gall  to  follow  Mrs.  Hoffman  home,  gaze  at  her  during  her  b: 
and  glower  at  her  from  her  bedside. 

She    finally    tired    of    this    ritual    and     confronted 
unwelcome  female  guest,  psychically,  face  to  face. 

"O.K.,  ghost,  I'm  stronger  than  you  are,"  she  said.  I 
compassionate.  You  are  not.  You  are  evil.  Leave  this  place, 
where  you  belong.  You  don't  belong  here." 

The  ghost,  seemingly  hesitant  for  a  moment,  was  repoi 
to  have  said,  "I'm  sorry."  Then  she  vanished  and  was  ni 
heard  from  again. 

"Exorcism  is  only  a  minor  part  of  witchcraft  and  mag 
Mrs.  Hoffman  explained.  "Actually  some  of  the  grea 
practitioners  of  magic  are  the  kahunas  or  Hawaiian  medii 
men.  Many  are  said  to  be  able  to  change  the  weather  and  I 
with  departed  spirits  who  live  again  in  other  forms." 

She  warned  of  the  psychic  vampires  among  us.  She  .< 
that  this  type  of  vampire  is  full  of  expressed  or  unexpres 
self-pity.  Such  a  person  drains  psychic  energy  from  everyi 
he  encounters,  leaving  his  victims  mentally  and  physio 
exhausted. 

Mrs.  Hoffman  noted  a  number  of  psychic  methods  t 
can  result  in  healing.  One  is  a  mind  control  method  in  wh 
people  are  trained  to  become  more  psychically  aware.  Migra 
headaches  have  reportedly  been  cured  through  this  process, 
another  method,  subjects  or  patients  are  taught  to  control  a 
bodily  function,  including  their  heartbeat,  so  that  severe  he 
attacks  can  be  automatically  averted. 

The  class  was  interested  in  the  theory  of  rein  earn  atk 
Mrs.  Hoffman,  who  is  quite  convinced  in  the  validity  of  tl 
theory  herself,  gave  the  students  a  quick  awareness  techniq 
for  mind  regression,  one  that  nearly  always  works. 

So  if  a  VVPI  student  suddenly  roars  like  a  lion  or  talks Ij 
William  the  Conqueror,  don't  blame  Aztec  Gold  or  jl 
Barleycorn.  And  don't  call  Worcester  State  Hospital 
probably  just  discovering  who  he  was.  .  .  .

ELLERY   E.    ROYAL,   '16 

Ellery  E.  Royal,  78,  former  superintendent  of 
the  Belmont  Home  in  Worcester,  died 
January  18,  1973  in  Sarasota,  Florida. 

He  was  born  in  Harvard,  Mass.  After 
graduating  as  a  mechanical  engineer,  he 
worked  for  Reed  &  Prince  Mfg.  Co.;  became 
president  of  U.S.  Screw  &  Stamping  Co.; 
and  later  became  affiliated  with  the  City  of 
Worcester  from  1932  until  1968. 

He  devised  mechanical  equipment  used 
by  patients  at  the  Belmont  Home  and  was 
responsible  for  the  transfer  from  the  Home 
Farm  to  Belmont  Home. 

Mr.  Royal  was  a  World  War  I  Army 
veteran  and  served  with  the  Ordnance 
Corps.  He  was  a  member  of  Phi  Sigma 
Kappa  and  a  former  member  of  the  Alumni 
Council. 

CLAYTON  T.    PIERCE,   '20 

Clayton  T.  Pierce,  77,  passed  away  Decem- 
ber 25,  1972  in  Massachusetts. 

He  was  born  in  Dalton,  Mass.,  on  Sept. 
28,  1895  and  later  studied  at  WPI  where  he 
was  a  member  of  Phi  Sigma  Kappa  Fra- 
ternity. 

Prior  to  his  retirement  he  was  a  self- 
employed  insurance  agent  in  Dalton. 

ALFRED    H.   WESSLEN.   '24 

Alfred  H.  Wesslen,  71,  a  retired  industrial 
engineer,  died  January  19,  1973  in 
Worcester. 

At  his  retirement  party,  held  in  1966,  he 
was  feted  by  the  Ware  Knitters  25-year  Club 
as  being  the  first  employee  on  the  payroll 
when  the  firm  was  founded  in  1  938. 

A  Worcester  native,  he  graduated  as  a 
mechanical  engineer  from  WPI  in  1924.  He 
worked  for  Crompton  &  Knowles;  Gilbert  & 
Barker  Co.,  West  Springfield;  U.S.  Finishing 
Co.,  Pawtucket,  R.I.;  American  Woolen  Co., 
Lawrence;  Ware  Valley  Manufacturing  Co., 
and  E-Z  Mills,  Bennington,  Vt.,  before  join- 
ing Ware  (Mass.)  Knitters,  Inc.,  as  a  con- 
sulting engineer. 

He  was  a  member  of  the  Republican  Town 
Committee  in  West  Brookfield. 

BURTON    E.    KELLEY,   '27 

Burton  E.  Kelley,  president  of  CBF  Systems, 
Inc.,  Covina,  Calif.,  died  on  January  4, 1 973. 
He  was  67. 

Mr.  Kelley  was  born  in  Springfield,  Mass., 
and  later  studied  at  WPI  where  he  was  a 
member  of  Phi  Gamma  Delta.  Prior  to  his 
latest  position,  he  had  been  associated  with 
Commonwealth  Management  Consultants; 
U.S.  Plywood-Champion  Papers,  Inc.; 
Ebasco  Services,  Inc.;  and  Best  Foods,  Inc.; 
all  of  New  York  City. 

He  was  past  president  of  the  American 
Society  of  Insurance  Management,  Inc.,  and 
served  as  an  insurance  lecturer  at  eight 
universities.  He  also  held  the  chairmanship 
of  the  American  Management  Association 
Insurance  Division  of  Conferences  and 
Seminars. 


CLARE   S.    RILEY,   '28 

Clare  S.  Riley,  67,  former  manager  of 
district  sales  with  Hartford  Empire,  now 
Emhart  Corp.,  Bloomfield,  Conn.,  died 
January  6,  1973  in  Clearwater,  Florida. 

He  was  born  in  Northampton,  Mass.  and 
later  studied  at  WPI.  During  his  career  he 
was  director  of  purchases  for  Metro  Glass 
Bottle  Division  of  National  Dairies  until  his 
retirement  in  1  970. 

Mr.  Riley  was  a  member  of  Alpha  Tau 
Omega. 

DONALD    C.    CONVERSE,   '31 

Donald  C.  Converse,  64,  died  on  January 
3  in  Hyannis,  Massachusetts  following  a 
long  illness. 

He  was  born  in  Bridgeport,  Conn.  After 
studying  at  WPI  and  MIT,  he  was  employed 
at  Builders  Iron  Foundry  in  Providence,  R.I., 
and  later  in  Philadelphia. 

He  then  moved  to  Hyannis  where  he 
worked  for  the  Colonial  Candle  Company. 
During  his  lifetime  he  also  owned  and 
operated  a  gift  shop  in  Hyannis  and  a 
restaurant  in  South  Yarmouth;  founded  the 
West  End  Businessmen's  Association  of 
Hyannis;  operated  his  own  construction 
business;  and  was  employed  as  a  mechanical 
engineer  at  Cape  Cod  Hospital. 

Mr.  Converse  was  a  member  of  Sigma 
Alpha  Epsilon  Fraternity  and  a  past  president 
of  the  Hyannis  Rotary  Club. 

CLINTON    E.    LEECH,   '36 

Clinton  E.  Leech  of  Summit,  New  Jersey 
recently  passed  away. 

A  mechanical  engineering  graduate,  he 
was  born  in  Springfield,  Mass.  on  May  27, 
1914.  At  WPI  he  was  a  member  of  Sigma 
Phi  Epsilon,  Tau  Beta  Pi,  and  Skull. 

For  many  years  he  was  with  the  Public 
Service  Electric  &  Gas  Co.,  Newark,  N.J., 
where  he  was  a  planning  and  consulting 
engineer. 

He  was  a  member  of  the  American  Gas 
Association,  the  New  Jersey  Gas  Associa- 
tion, and  the  Society  of  Gas  Operators,  New 
York  City. 

PAUL  M.    RUSSO,  '71 

Paul  M.  Russo,  23,  died  on  January  1 ,  1 973 
in  Thorndike,  Massachusetts. 

He  was  born  in  Palmer.  In  1971  he  re- 
ceived his  BS  in  civil  engineering  from  WPI 
where  he  was  a  member  of  Phi  Kappa 
Theta  Fraternity. 

He  was  a  member  of  the  American  Society 
of  Civil  Engineers  and  was  employed  by 
Tighe  &  Bond,  Inc.,  Holyoke,  Mass.

porate vice  president  in  charge  of  engineer- 
ing and  purchasing.  Diamond  International 
Corporation,  and  will  be  headquartered  in 
Stamford,  Conn.  In  1956  he  joined  Diamond

New  York  Division.  Later  he  was  a  manager,

Camp  Dresser  &  McKee,  Inc.,  environ- 
mental consulting  engineers.  Recently  he 
was  director  of  the  Washington  Water  Re- 
sources Management  Administration,  which 
is  responsible  for  water  and  wastewater

ipal engineering  in  the  area,  in  addition  to 
responsibilities  in  connection  with  CDM 
federal  and  international  projects.  In  1958 
Freese  received  the  National  Capital  Award 
as  the  outstanding  young  engineer  of  the 
year.

Where  Are  You  ?  ? 

With  the  current  roster  of  WPI  alumni  at 
around  10,300,  it's  probably  inevitable  that 
we  can't  keep  track  of  them  all.  There  are 
some  300  or  so  alumni  whose  whereabouts 
we  don't  know,  and  this  means  that  we 
can't  send  them  the  WPI  Journal  or  WPI 
Newsbriefs.  The  list  on  this  page  represents 
about  half  the  people  we've  lost  touch  with. 
If  you  can  give  us  information  to  help  locate 
any  of  them,  we  would  be  most  grateful. 
(The  second  half  of  the  list  will  run  in  the 
August  issue.)

PHILIP  B.  CROMMELIN  has  been  named 
product  manager,  utility  precipitators,  at 
Research-Cottrell,  Inc.,  Bound  Brook,  N.J. 
In  his  newly  created  position  he  will  ad- 
minister and  promote  liaison  between 
customers  and  company  field  personnel. 
Since  joining  Research-Cottrell  in  1954  he 
has  been  service  engineer,  application  engi- 
neer, chief  products  engineer  and  manager 
of  turnkey  operations.  He  is  a  member  of  the 
Air  Pollution  Control  Association.

1955 


ROBERT  E.  OLSON  has  been  named 
manager  of  the  fluid  dynamics  laboratory  at 
United  Aircraft  Research  Laboratories,  East 
Hartford,  Conn.  He  will  be  responsible  for  a 
variety  of  research  programs  related  to  gas 
turbine  engines,  noise  reduction  and  pollu- 
tion control.  Previously  he  was  chief  of  the 
gas  dynamics  section.  .  .  .  KENNETH  H. 
RUSSELL,  a  chemical  engineer  with  the 
ammunition  development  and  engineering 
directorate,  Picatinny  Arsenal,  Dover,  N.J., 
was  recently  presented  a  special  achieve- 
ment award  for  "exemplary  performance  of 
duty"  while  assigned  to  Radford  Army  Am- 
munition Depot.  As  a  member  of  a  "should 
cost  study  team,"  his  recommendations  for 
improved  operations  resulted  in  a  govern- 
ment saving  of  $3.5  million. 


1956 


PHILIP  P.  BEDARD  is  a  research  associate 
at  Nova  University,  Dania,  Fla.  .  .  .  CHARLES 
E.  GUNN  has  been  appointed  Mayor  of 
North  Branford,  Conn.  He  has  served  as 
deputy  mayor  since  being  elected  to  the 
council  in  1971  and  was  designated  mayor 
to  complete  the  unexpired  term  of  the  pre- 
vious mayor  until  town  elections  are  held 
this  year.  Charlie  writes  that  his  new  post 
is  part-time  and  unpaid  and  that  he  keeps 
"bread  on  the  table"  by  continuing  as  senior 
test  engineer  at  United  Illuminating  Co.,  in 
New  Haven.  .  .  .  GEORGE  P.  STROM  was 
recently  appointed  director  of  manufacturing 
of  Ortho  Diagnostics  in  Raritan,  N.J.  He  will 
be  responsible  for  biochemical  production, 
processing,  packaging,  and  process  develop- 
ment. Strom  joined  Johnson  &  Johnson  in 
Chicago  in  1967  as  superintendent  of  dis- 
tribution. Since  1970  he  has  held  his 
present  position  as  chief  engineer,  Eastern

Hear!  Hear! 


a  cii 


In  1 961  Ralph  Guertin  received  his  BS  in  physics  from  WPI  where  he  graduated  summa 
laude  at  the  head  of  his  class.  By  1969  he  had  completed  his  doctoral  thesis  at  Yale.  Tod 
he  can  be  found  teaching  theoretical  physics  to  graduate  students  at  Rice  University 
Houston,  Texas. 

A  nice  success  story  as  far  as  it  goes.  An  outstanding  student  has  developed  into  an  on 
standing  professor. 

But  Dr.  Guertin's  story  is  more  than  a  success.  It  is  a  miracle.  He  is  the  first  totally  de 
person  in  this  country,  and  possibly  the  world,  to  hold  a  full-time  professorship  in  a  hearii 
college. 

How  can  a  man  who  lost  his  hearing  at  the  age  of  seven  hold  such  a  demanding  position 
one  that  requires  the  verbal  give-and-take  intrinsic  in  a  classroom  situation? 

"It's  because  of  the  women  in  my  life,"  he  says.  "Whatever  success  I  have  had  is  due 
them," 

He  admits  quite  candidly  that  he  owes  his  normal  speaking  voice  to  his  mother,  Mrs.  Irei 
Nash  Guertin.  After  a  disastrous  childhood  bout  with  meningitis  and  measles  which  result' 
in  his  hearing  loss,  it  was  she  who  kept  him  talking  naturally  and  corrected  him  when  he  errs 

According  to  his  colleagues  at  Rice  University,  today  Dr.  Guertin  speaks  normally  durii 
his  lectures.  He  even  finds  time  for  spirited  question-and-answer  periods  answering  whatev 
questions  his  students  may  fire  at  him.  (An  A+  for  Mrs.  Guertin  and  her  speech  lessons!) 

Another  woman  who  helped  him  along  the  road  to  success  was  Miss  Chiyona  Kinoshi 
who  taught  him  lip-reading  at  Clarke  School  for  the  Deaf  in  Northampton,  Mass.  When  I 
entered  the  school  in  1  947  he  knew  nothing  about  lip-reading  whatsoever  and  at  first  refusi 
to  try  it,  always  insisting  that  his  hearing  would  return  some  day.  But  Miss  Kinoshita  w. 
patient,  determined,  and  compassionate.  In  the  end  she  won  and  so  did  young  Ralph  Guerti 

Now  he  reads  lips  so  proficiently  that  he  often  has  the  feeling  that  he  can  actually  he; 
And  people  with  whom  he  is  speaking  are  astonished  because  after  they  say  a  couple 
words,  he  can  comprehend  the  whole  sentence  before  they  finish. 

His  academic  excellence  and  his  unusual  ability  to  communicate  led  him  to  a  number* 
interesting  jobs  after  his  graduation  from  WPI.  For  several  summers  he  worked  as  a  solid  stff 
physicist  at  the  U.S.  Naval  Ordnance  Laboratory  in  Maryland  and  at  the  National  Bureaui 
Standards  in  Washington,  DC.  Later  he  served  as  a  research  assistant  in  the  physics  depar 
ment  at  the  Middle  East  Technical  University  at  Ankara,  Turkey,  and  as  a  research  assistB 
at  the  University  of  Nijmegen  in  The  Netherlands. 

Language  was  no  barrier.  He  learned  Turkish  and  could  lip-read  it  in  no  time.  He  diol 
have  to  learn  Dutch  because  they  spoke  English  at  the  university  where  he  was  employe® 

Dr.  Guertin  can  also  lip-read  French.  He  is  even  able  to  understand  an  Argentine  studl 
in  one  of  his  classes  who  speaks  with  a  Spanish  accent. 

Oddly,  the  toughest  accent  for  him  to  lip-read  is  British.  He  says,  "It  shows  on  their  lips 

Following  his  stint  in  Europe  and  after  receiving  his  PhD  from  Yale,  he  became  a  pc 
doctoral  fellow  at  the  Lawrence  Radiation  Laboratory  at  the  University  of  California  at  Berke 
While  there,  Prof.  Geoffrey  Chew  recommended  him  to  officials  at  Rice. 

Any  doubts  the  Rice  representatives  may  have  had  about  their  new  teaching  prosp 
promptly  disappeared  after  they  heard  him  deliver  a  lecture.  They  were,  in  a  word,  impresi 

Any  lingering  doubts  that  Dr.  Guertin  may  have  had  also  have  disappeared  since  he 
been  at  the  university.  He  is  quite  convinced  that  he  has  no  further  communication  probl 
to  surmount.  He  plans  to  make  teaching  his  career. 

Should  he  waver,  he  has  two  more  ladies  to  turn  to.  One  is  his  wife,  Nu  Nu  Mae,  whom 
met  at  Yale  while  she  was  earning  her  masler's  in  international  relations  and  teaching 
native  Burmese.  And  the  other  is  their  four-year-old  daughter  who  was  born  in  The  Neth 
lands. 

As  things  stand  now,  it  looks  as  though  Dr.  Ralph  Guertin  has  already  decided  exac 
where  he  is  going  and  how  to  get  there.  If,  indeed,  he  isn't  there  already.

NOAA  ships  Rude  and  Heck  according  to  a

Oceanic  and  Atmospheric  Administration  of 
the  U.S.  Department  of  Commerce.  The 
90-ft.  sister  ships  are  the  only  ones  of  their 
kind  in  the  nation  designed  to  obtain  in- 
formation through  wire  dragging  for  under- 
water navigational  hazards  such  as  sunken 
wrecks,  rocks,  etc.  Pickens  assumed  com- 
mand of  the  ships  on  which  he  was  previously 
executive  officer,  at  the  Atlantic  Marine 
Center,  Norfolk,  Va.

Heald's  newest  development,  a 
rotary  electro-chemical  machining 
process,  offers  industry  a  practical 
way  to  perform  precision  machin- 
ing operations  on  "difficult  to  ma- 
chine" conductive  materials  such 
as  high  strength,  high  temperature 
alloys.  Using  electro-chemical  ma- 
chining, stock  removal   rates  are 


unaffected  by  material  hardness  so 
production  rates  are  substantially 
increased.

will  be  spurred  on  by  the  chal- 
lenge of  the  1970's  and  the  need 
to  meet  the  ever-changing  require- 
ments of  our  shrinking  world.

VESA  I.  AUNIO  works  at  General  Electric

JACK  H.  McCABE,  '68  and  RONALD  J.  GORDON.  '67  recently  joined  forces  and  came  up 
with  some  leading  edge  work  that  has  led  to  the  implementation  of  a  computer  package, 
Mini-Plan,  that  is  now  being  sold  by  IBM  to  businesses  throughout  the  country. 

The  work  was  performed  at  Hammond  Plastics,  Inc.,  Worcester,  where  McCabe  is  director 
of  management  information  systems. 

Their  accomplishment  is  considered  significant  because  it  is  the  first  time  that  a  small 
company  with  a  small  computer  has  been  able  to  perform  the  same  type  of  financial  analysis 
previously  attempted  only  by  large  companies  with  large  computers.  In  fact,  practical  planning 
models  at  Hammond  can  now  be  implemented  on  a  System/3  computer  as  well  as  on  a  more 
recently  initiated  disc-based  version. 

At  first  it  wasn't  known  if  such  a  plan  was  feasible.  But  when  Hammond  decided  to  estab- 
lish a  corporate  objective  of  increasing  revenues  by  20  percent  across  the  board  in  1971,  it 
looked  to  its  System/3  as  a  possible  tool  to  reduce  the  repetitive  manual  calculations  involved. 

Gordon,  who  is  a  systems  engineer  for  IBM,  developed  the  conceptual  model  for  Hammond 
Plastics'  income  projections. 

"We  proved  this  on  a  System/360  model  50,"  says  McCabe.  "And  then  we  developed 
simplified,  open-ended  routines  to  express  the  profit  and  loss  parameters  in  the  English-like 
language  of  System/3.  Ultimately  we  were  able  to  program  balance  and  cash  flow  projections 
directly  in  the  RPG  II  language  without  any  need  for  conversion." 

Now  Hammond  has  converted  to  a  disc  system  and  implemented  Mini- Plan  which  offers 
a  simple  method  for  organizing  planning  data,  for  establishing  computational  logic  to  apply 
to  the  data,  and  for  generating  reports. 

According  to  McCabe  Mini-Plan  makes  it  easier  to  make  inquiries  and  extends  the  range 
of  "what-if"  questions  the  company  can  pose  to  the  system.  One  such  "what-if"  question 
resulted  in  a  $5,000  savings  on  a  resins  order  that  Hammond  made. 

The  accuracy  of  the  company's  new  financial  models  runs  to  within  3  to  5  percent  of  their 
projections.  "This  is  accurate  enough  to  be  extremely  useful,"  McCabe  concludes. 

Plastics  World  and  dp/Solutions  recently  featured  articles  highlighting  the  work  done  by 
McCabe  and  Gordon.

Covers 

Congratulations  on  your  December  1972 
issue.  When  I  received  it,  I  set  it  aside 
thinking  it  was  an  international  magazine  to 
which  I  subscribe,  and  which  I  usually  take 
along  on  business  trips  to  read  more  thor- 
oughly. Imagine  my  surprise  to  open  it  on 
the  Metroliner  to  Washington  and  learn  the 
significance  of  the  flags  and  our  exchange 
students  from  City  University. 

I  read  every  word  of  the  students'  com- 
ments, because  I  love  London,  and  we  have 
entertained  British  friends  who  had  never 
been  in  America  and  they  also  had  some 
weird  ideas  about  our  country. 

I  also  enjoy  your  "Feedback"  section. 
Good  old  Tech  is  certainly  becoming  a 
leader  in  engineering  education. 

Arthur  C.  Manning,  '27 
Upper  Montclair,  N.J.

shared  my  interest  in  the  program,  as| 
have  four  colleges  in  the  immediate  ai 
He  asked  me  if  it  would  be  possible 
obtain  a  copy  of  this  article  for  him,  i 
this  is  the  purpose  of  my  writing  to  you.

The  Norton  Spirit. 


That's  the  name  we've  chosen 
for  the  championship  race  car  we'll 
be  campaigning  on  some  of  the 
world's  toughest  testing  grounds. . . 
a  lean,  tough  rolling  projectile 
crammed  to  the  throttle  with  850 
horses  of  double  overhead  cam 
turbo-super-charged  power! 

Sponsored  by  various  divisions 
of  Norton  Company  whose  products 
are  closely  linked  to  the  manufac- 
ture and  fabrication  of  most  auto- 
motive parts,  this  sleek  racing 


Norton  grinding  wheels  are 

depended  on  for  precision 

grinding  and  finishing  of  critical 

engine  components,  transmission 

parts,  drive  shafts,  steering  gear 

and  front  wheel  suspension  parts. 

Norton  coated  abrasive  belts, 
sheets  and  discs  play  an 
important  part  in  grinding  brake 
shoes,  crankshaft  lapping  and  in 
the  preparation  and  finishing  of 
all  types  of  metal  and  fiberglass 
automotive  panels. 

Because  they  grip  curved  and 
irregular  surfaces  tightly  and 
strip  off  quickly  and  cleanly, 
Norton  pressure  sensitive  mask- 
ing tapes  are  used  extensively 
in  the  preparation  and  final 
painting  of  automotive  bodies. 


machine  will  hittheU.S.A.C.  cham- 
pionship trail  from  Indianapolis  to 
Ontario,  from  the  Pocono  500  to 
the  Michigan  200. 

Our  entry  in  this  internationally 
renowned  sports  event  underscores 
the  important  role  Norton  products 
play  in  the  automotive  industry.  It 
also  reflects  the  combined  talents, 
skills  and  competitive  spirit  of 
Norton's  1 8,000  employees.  It  is  this 
same  spirit  of  endeavor  and  a 
genuine  desire  to  excel  that  has 


made  Norton  the  world's  largest 
and  best  known  manufacturer  of 
abrasives  and  a  prime  supplier  of 
industrial  ceramics,  pressure- 
sensitive  tapes  and  plastics  and 
synthetics. 

Keep  your  eye  on  the  new 
Norton  Spirit. . .  an  exciting  symbol 
of  a  company  and  people  that  are 
on  the  move!  Norton  Company; 
World  Headquarters,  Worcester, 
Massachusetts  01 606. 


Norton  industrial  ceramics, 
including  refractory  cements, 
are  widely  used  to  line  melting 
furnaces  in  which  automotive 
engine  blocks  and  other 
components  are  cast. 

Norton  abrasive  materials  are 
selected  for  their  long  life  and 
increased  productivity  on  a 
variety  of  automotive  produc- 
tion applications  including  the 
deburring  and  finishing  of  gears, 
carburetor  parts,  connecting 
rods,  ball  joints,  butterfly 
valves  and  thermostats. 

Norton  TYGON®  tubing  is 
ideal  for  low  pressure  fuel, 
vent  and  refueling  lines  where 
high  visibility  is  required.
